vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2, synonyms: Meticorten, Winpred, Deltasone, etc.)

1. Physico-chemical profile (primary drivers for bioaccumulation)

• Molecular weight: 358.4 g mol-1  
• logKOW (neutral species, 25 °C): 1.5–1.8 (PubChem CID 5865; ECHA disseminated dossier)  
• Water solubility: 100–200 mg L-1 (slightly soluble, but clearly more soluble than classical lipophiles)  
• pKa (acidic OH): ≈12.3 – remains predominantly non-ionised under physiological pH (7.4)  
• Vapour pressure: 1.1 × 10-9 Pa (negligible – inhalation exposure to vapour is irrelevant)  
• Predicted BCF (BCFBAF v3.02, EPA): 11–25 L kg-1 (well below the 2 000 L kg-1 “bioaccumulative” threshold in REACH and OECD criteria).

Interpretation: The combination of a low logKOW (<2) and a low predicted BCF already points to a low intrinsic tendency to partition into neutral lipids or adipose tissue.

2. Human ADME data (systemic disposition)

Absorption  
• Oral bioavailability is high (≈80 %), but prednisone is a pro-drug; it is rapidly converted (~90 %) to prednisolone by hepatic 11β-HSD-1.

Distribution  
• Volume of distribution (Vd) for prednisolone: 0.4–0.7 L kg-1 (Derendorf et al., 1993; EMCDDA drug monograph). Values only slightly above total body water indicate limited sequestration in adipose tissue.  
• Plasma protein binding: 70–90 % to albumin and corticosteroid-binding globulin (saturable; free fraction increases at high doses), favouring systemic circulation rather than tissue storage.  
• Prednisone/prednisolone readily cross the placenta and appear in breast milk (low levels, short residence), showing the ability to distribute but not necessarily accumulate.

Metabolism  
• Phase I: rapid hepatic reduction and oxidation (11β-HSD, CYP3A4).  
• Phase II: extensive glucuronidation and sulfation (UGT2B, SULT2A).  
• Result: multiple polar metabolites with greatly reduced logKOW (≤-0.5) → rapid elimination.

Excretion  
• 60–80 % of an oral dose is eliminated in urine within 24 h, chiefly as conjugated metabolites (Thompson et al., 1970).  
• Terminal elimination half-life (plasma):  
  – Prednisone ≈3–4 h  
  – Prednisolone ≈2–3 h  
• Biological (pharmacodynamic) half-life is longer (12–36 h) because of receptor occupancy, not physical retention.

3. Empirical tissue concentration data

• In therapeutic settings (5–60 mg day-1), trough plasma concentrations of free prednisolone are typically <100 ng mL-1; steady-state is reached in ≤48 h; no progressive increase on chronic dosing provided liver/kidney function remains normal (Almawi & Melemedjian, 2002).  
• Repeated-dose animal studies (rat, dog) show no preferential accumulation in fat or liver after 4–13 weeks (ECHA study summaries, 2023). Tissue:plasma ratios ≤2.  
• Autopsy data in humans receiving long-term corticosteroids do not report measurable depot levels in fat; rather, systemic effects relate to pharmacodynamics, not accumulation.

4. Mechanistic aspects limiting bioaccumulation

• logKOW <2 makes partitioning into neutral lipids thermodynamically unfavourable.  
• Rapid, high-capacity phase II conjugation prevents saturation, even in high-dose regimens.  
• High plasma protein binding keeps most circulating drug in a reversible state, further limiting depot formation.  
• Short elimination half-life produces negligible steady-state body burden (Accumulation index ≈1.3 for 8-h dosing interval, using t½ = 3 h).

5. Persistence in the body

• Chemical persistence (parent compound) is low; <5 % parent prednisone remains 6 h post-dose.  
• Active metabolite (prednisolone) is also transient; steady-state free levels return to baseline within 24 h of cessation.  
• Conjugated metabolites have no known pharmacological activity and are quickly excreted.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion

• All physical–chemical parameters, predictive BCF models, human PK data and empirical tissue studies support LOW bioaccumulation potential.  
• No evidence of long-term storage in adipose tissue or other depots.  
• Systemic persistence is governed by receptor binding, not chemical sequestration; once dosing stops, plasma and tissue concentrations fall rapidly (hours to a day).  
• Accordingly, prednisone is not considered a POP, PBT or vPvB substance under REACH/ECHA criteria.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps

• Limited direct human tissue measurements (e.g., adipose biopsy) after chronic therapy.  
• Data largely derive from therapeutic doses; accidental massive overdoses have not been systematically examined for depot formation, although rapid metabolism is still expected.  
• Patients with severe hepatic impairment may show prolonged half-life, but accumulation is still modest because the drug is dialyzable and other metabolic pathways can compensate.

8. Overall classification

• Systemic availability (parent + active metabolite): HIGH (due to good absorption and distribution).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent glucocorticoid).  
• Bioaccumulation potential/persistence: LOW.

Therefore, prednisone does not pose a significant risk of bioaccumulation in human tissues under either therapeutic or incidental exposure scenarios.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Bioaccumulation Assessment  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachloro­dibenzodioxin (TCDD)  
Synonyms: “Dioxin”, TC-Dioxin, TetraCDD  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

1. Chemical and Physico-chemical Drivers  
• Log Kow: 6.8–7.2 (Hansch et al., 1993; ECHA) ⟶ very lipophilic  
• Water solubility: 20–50 ng L-1 at 25 °C (EPA, 2010) ⟶ extremely low  
• Vapor pressure: 3–5 × 10-10 Pa (Howard & Meylan, 1997) ⟶ negligible volatility  
Interpretation: The combination of very high Kow and vanishing water solubility strongly favours partitioning into neutral lipids of biota and makes passive elimination slow.

2. Absorption and Systemic Availability  
• Oral absorption: 40–60 % in rodents and non-human primates; human data modelled at ~50 % (Kreuzer et al., 1997).  
• Inhalation: Limited direct data; particles containing TCDD are efficiently deposited in alveoli; uptake is assumed high (>75 %) for respirable fractions.  
• Dermal: <10 % in rhesus monkey and guinea pig (Brewster & Martin, 1990).  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH for oral and inhalation pathways; LOW-to-MEDIUM for dermal.

3. Distribution  
• Rapid association with blood lipoproteins → redistribution to highly perfused tissues (liver, adipose, muscle).  
• In humans, ≥90 % of body burden is in adipose tissue; liver holds ~5–10 % bound to CYP1A2 (Birnbaum, 1994).  
• Placental transfer demonstrated; cord-blood levels ≈25–50 % of maternal plasma (Dewailly et al., 2000).  
• Excretion in breast milk (milk-fat concentrations proportional to maternal body burden).

4. Metabolism and Elimination  
• Very slowly metabolised via CYP1A1/1A2 to polar hydroxylated metabolites; rate-limiting.  
• Enterohepatic circulation recycles parent compound and metabolites.  
• Major elimination route in humans: faeces (biliary excretion of parent + metabolites).  
• Whole-body human half-life: 5.8–11.3 years (geometric mean ≈7.5 years) based on accident-related and background cohorts (Pirkle et al., 1989; Kreuzer et al., 1997; Milbrath et al., 2009).  
• Children eliminate faster (half-life ≈2–4 years) due to higher growth dilution and metabolism.

5. Empirical Evidence of Bioaccumulation  
• Biomonitoring: General population blood lipid levels 2–7 pg g-1 lipid; occupationally exposed up to 10³–10⁴ pg g-1.  
• Vietnam veterans (Operation Ranch Hand): cumulative body burdens 100–400 ng kg-1 body weight decades after exposure (Michalek & Tripathi, 1999).  
• BCF/BAF (fish): 10⁴–10⁶ (USEPA, 2010) – although aquatic values, they illustrate biomagnification potential up food chains reaching humans.  
• Half-life correlations with body-fat content confirm sequestration in lipids and slow release (Aylward et al., 2005).

6. Weight-of-Evidence Conclusion  
a) Potential for accumulation in tissues: HIGH  
   – Physico-chemical profile (log Kow >6) predicts strong lipid partitioning.  
   – Human biomonitoring demonstrates lifelong retention in adipose and liver.  
b) Persistence in the human body: HIGH  
   – Elimination half-life ≈7 years in adults.  
   – Limited metabolic clearance; enterohepatic recycling prolongs residence.  
c) Systemic availability: HIGH (oral/inhalation) → fuels body burden; dermal uptake lower but relevant for heavily contaminated soils or liquids.  

7. Data Gaps and Uncertainties  
• Direct human kinetic data rely on accidental or occupational cohorts; ethical constraints preclude controlled dosing studies.  
• Variability in half-life due to age, body-fat changes, breastfeeding, and genetics (CYP1A2 polymorphisms) introduces uncertainty in individual predictions.  
• Limited information on newer populations with lower background exposure—half-lives may differ slightly with reduced ongoing intake.

8. Overall Bioaccumulation Classification  
Using REACH and WHO criteria (BCF >5000 or human t½ >1 year):  
Bioaccumulation potential: VERY HIGH (vB substance).  
Toxicodynamic activity (AhR agonism, endocrine modulation): HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH (oral/inhalation); LOW–MEDIUM (dermal)

Therefore, 2,3,7,8-TCDD is a prototypical persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT/vPvB) chemical. Continued dietary intake—even at background levels—leads to measurable body burdens over a lifetime, while episodic high exposures result in decades-long internal persistence.

tests.prompt: Assess the bioaccumulation potential of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Discuss its potential for accumulation in tissues and its persistence in the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Benzene  
CAS No. 71-43-2 (synonyms: cyclohexatriene, benzol)

Question:  What is the potential of benzene to bioaccumulate in the human body (i.e. to build-up in tissues and to persist once exposure ceases)?

1. Key physicochemical parameters relevant to bioaccumulation

• Molecular weight: 78.11 g mol-1  
• log Kow (25 °C): 2.13 – 2.30 (ECHA, PubChem)  
• Water solubility: 1.8 – 1.9 g L-1  
• Vapour pressure: 12.7 kPa at 25 °C  
• Henry’s constant: 0.0056 – 0.0065 atm m³ mol-1

Interpretation:  A log Kow < 3 and high volatility favour rapid elimination rather than storage in lipids. These values place benzene in the “low bioaccumulation concern” domain according to REACH Annex XIII and OECD 305 guidance.

2. Human toxicokinetic data

Absorption  
• Inhalation: 45–75 % of inspired benzene is taken up in the lungs at rest (ATSDR 2007; Pekari et al. 1992).  
• Oral: almost completely absorbed (> 90 %) in volunteers (Snyder et al. 1975).  
• Dermal: 0.1–0.5 mg cm-2 h-1 flux; ≈ 0.1–2 % systemic uptake during short contact (Jo et al. 2004).

Distribution  
• Rapid distribution to well-perfused organs; appreciable partitioning into adipose because of moderate lipophilicity (fat:blood ratios 2–3 in rodents; Sato et al. 1974).  
• However, equilibrium is reached quickly (minutes to hours) and maintained only while exposure continues.

Metabolism / Biotransformation  
• > 90 % of absorbed benzene is oxidised in the liver (mainly CYP2E1) to phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, trans,trans-muconic acid and S-phenyl-mercapturic acid (Henderson et al. 1997).  
• Phase-II conjugation (sulfate/glucuronide) further increases polarity.

Excretion  
• Unmetabolised benzene: exhaled air; elimination half-life 1–2 h in humans (Nomiyama & Nomiyama 1974).  
• Metabolites: > 80 % eliminated in urine within 24 h; urinary half-lives 6–15 h (Schnatter et al. 2010).  
• Very small amounts retained longer than 24 h (trace levels detectable up to 48–72 h).

3. Empirical evidence for tissue retention or accumulation

Human studies  
• Breath-analysis after single occupational exposures shows biphasic elimination: fast phase ≈ 1 h, slow phase ≈ 12–24 h, consistent with redistribution from fat but not long-term storage (Pekari et al. 1992).  
• Post-mortem measurements in fat of workers with chronic exposure reveal benzene below detection (< 0.02 mg kg^-1) within hours after death, indicating no long-term depot (Wallace 1996).  
• Bone-marrow “targeting” is often cited for benzene’s haematotoxicity, but this reflects metabolic activation in marrow rather than accumulation of the parent compound.

Animal data  
• Repeated inhalation (300 ppm, rats, 4 weeks): no progressive increase in fat or blood concentrations between day 1 and day 28 (Sabourin et al. 1988).  
• Total body burden decreases to < 1 % of dose 48 h post-exposure (Sabourin et al. 1987).

4. Model predictions

• BCF (human whole body) estimated by EPISuite BCFBAF v3.02: 10 – 17 (well below the 2 000 cut-off for concern).  
• Human PBPK models (Tardif et al. 1997; Brown et al. 1998) reproduce observed data and predict steady-state fat concentrations of ≈ 1.3 times blood during continuous 1 ppm exposure; after exposure stops, 95 % is cleared in < 12 h.

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation

Criteria pointing to LOW bioaccumulation  
• log Kow < 3, MW < 500, high volatility.  
• Rapid oxidative metabolism, mainly to hydrophilic metabolites with urinary excretion t½ ≤ 15 h.  
• PBPK and empirical data show > 90 % dose cleared within 24 h.  
• No evidence of progressive increase in tissue levels under repeated exposure in animals or humans.

Potential factors that might suggest some accumulation  
• Moderate fat:blood partition (≈ 3) means transient storage in adipose; yields a minor “slow phase” of elimination (∼12 h).  
• Continuous high-level exposure can maintain a quasi-steady-state in fat, but equilibrium is rapidly lost when exposure ceases.

6. Persistence in the body

• Parent benzene: biological half-life in blood ≈ 1 h (fast) and ≈ 12 h (slow).  
• Metabolites: cleared predominantly within 24 h; no long-term sequestration demonstrated.  
Hence benzene is considered non-persistent in humans.

7. Classification

Systemic availability: High (because absorption is efficient by inhalation and oral routes).  
Bioaccumulation potential: Low.  
Persistence in tissues after exposure ceases: Low.

8. Uncertainties / data gaps

• Limited direct tissue concentration data in chronically exposed humans; reliance on PBPK modelling.  
• Genetic and lifestyle factors (e.g., CYP2E1 polymorphisms, alcohol) can modify metabolism rates, but not expected to turn benzene into a highly persistent compound.  
• Co-exposures that inhibit metabolism could transiently increase blood/brain levels, but clearance still occurs within a day.

9. Conclusion

Benzene is readily absorbed but is equally rapidly metabolised and excreted. Its moderate lipophilicity allows only short-term partitioning into adipose tissue, with a terminal elimination half-life on the order of hours, not days or weeks. All available human, animal, and modelling data converge on the conclusion that benzene has a low potential to bioaccumulate or persist in the human body under typical exposure scenarios.

Key sources  
• ATSDR (2007). Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
• ECHA IUCLID & Dissemination dossier (2023 access).  
• Pekari K et al. (1992). Int Arch Occup Environ Health 63: 59-68.  
• Henderson RF et al. (1997). Environ Health Perspect 105(S1): 1205-1211.  
• Sabourin PJ et al. (1988). Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 94: 128-140.  
• Tardif R et al. (1997). Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 142: 311-322.  
• EPISuite v4.11 (US EPA, 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Bioaccumulation assessment for:  
Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic; CAS 15502-74-6; synonyms include arsenious acid, AsO33–, As(III))

1. Key physicochemical and ADME characteristics
   • Water-soluble inorganic ion/oxyanion; log Kow is not applicable.  
   • Exists predominantly as undissociated arsenious acid (H3AsO3) at pH < 9.2 and as H2AsO3– / HAsO32– above that pH.  
   • Rapid gastrointestinal (≈ 80–95 %) and pulmonary absorption in humans and laboratory animals (ATSDR, 2007; WHO, 2001).  
   • Limited plasma protein binding; distributes quickly to liver, kidney, skin, lung, and to a lesser extent brain (Fowler et al., 2009).  
   • Undergoes enzymatic biomethylation to monomethylarsonic acid (MMA) and dimethylarsinic acid (DMA) which markedly increases urinary clearance (Vahter, 2002).  
   • Main elimination route = urine (60–80 % of an absorbed dose within 48–72 h), with minor fecal and biliary contributions (ATSDR, 2007).  

2. Tissue retention and biological half-lives
   • Whole blood t1/2 ~ 10 h for inorganic As(III); total body t1/2 (fast compartment) 1–2 days (Buchet et al., 1981; ATSDR, 2007).  
   • Slow compartment (keratin-rich tissues) t1/2 estimated at 1–3 weeks in skin and ~ 3–6 months in hair and nails, reflecting protein binding rather than continuous circulation (WHO, 2001; IARC, 2012).  
   • No evidence for long-term storage in lipid depots (arsenite is not lipophilic).  

3. Observations from biomonitoring studies
   • Occupational cohorts exposed to As(III) in smelters or pesticide production show elevated urinary total arsenic while exposure continues; levels fall rapidly (within days) after exposure cessation (Terasaka et al., 2004; ATSDR, 2007).  
   • Hair and nail arsenic concentrations remain elevated for months after short-term high exposure, confirming binding to keratin but not ongoing systemic circulation (Feng et al., 2015).  
   • No progressive concentration with age has been observed in blood or internal soft tissues of the general population, even in high-arsenic drinking-water areas, provided exposure is constant (Yuan et al., 2018).  

4. Comparison with bioaccumulative substances
   • OECD and REACH regard BCF > 2000 L kg-1 in biota and long biological half-lives as indicators of high bioaccumulation. Arsenite does not meet these criteria: human BCF equivalents (ratio of tissue conc. to daily intake) are < 1 for most soft organs due to rapid clearance.  
   • Unlike persistent organic pollutants (POPs) or methylmercury, arsenite is not stored in fat and is efficiently biotransformed to water-soluble metabolites.

5. Uncertainties / data gaps
   • Few quantitative human half-life data for individual organs; reliance on urinary excretion studies.  
   • Genetic polymorphisms in arsenic methyltransferase (AS3MT) can lengthen residence time in susceptible individuals.  
   • Co-exposure to selenium, nutritional status, and disease states (e.g., kidney impairment) may modify retention.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – rapid and extensive absorption via oral and inhalation routes.  
   • Persistence in most soft tissues: LOW to MODERATE (hours to days).  
   • Long-term sequestration: LIMITED to keratinized tissues (hair, nails, skin) where it is incorporated into proteins; this does not constitute an internal reservoir capable of remobilisation.  
   • Overall bioaccumulation potential in humans: LOW. Arsenite does not progressively build up in internal organs under intermittent exposure because it is efficiently methylated and excreted, although biomarkers in hair/nails can remain elevated for months.

Therefore, while arsenite has high acute systemic availability and significant toxicodynamic activity, its propensity to bioaccumulate in human tissues is low. The principal health concern arises from continuous or repeated exposure rather than from long-term retention of past doses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Bioaccumulation potential of Methylmercury  
CAS 16056-34-1 (common synonyms: monomethylmercury, MeHg⁺, methylmercuric ion)

1. Key physicochemical and kinetic parameters  
• Molecular formula/weight: CH₃Hg⁺ (215 g mol⁻¹ for CH₃HgCl)  
• Charge: cationic, forms neutral complexes with anions or with cysteine (CH₃Hg-S-Cys)  
• Log Kow (for CH₃HgCl): 1.4–1.7 (Hudson et al., 1994). Although this log Kow is modest, classical lipophilicity metrics underestimate bioaccumulation because MeHg binds with high affinity to thiol-containing proteins.  
• Protein binding: >95 % to sulfhydryl groups in plasma and tissues (Clarkson & Magos, 2006).  
• Volume of distribution (Vd): 1.0–1.5 L kg⁻¹ in adults (WHO, 2007) – indicates extensive tissue distribution.  
• Biological half-life in humans: most studies converge on ≈50 (±10) days for whole-body burden, although estimates up to 80–90 days exist; fetal and brain compartments can retain MeHg longer (Smith & Farris, 1996; Berglund et al., 2005).

2. Absorption (systemic availability)  
• Oral: 90–95 % of dietary MeHg is absorbed in the small intestine (Rowland et al., 1983).  
• Inhalation: low relevance for the cation, but aerosolised species would also be efficiently absorbed.  
• Dermal: negligible compared with oral.  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH.

3. Distribution and accumulation in humans  
• Blood: MeHg binds predominantly to hemoglobin; erythrocyte:plasma ratio ≈10:1.  
• Central nervous system (CNS): The MeHg-cysteine conjugate uses the L-type amino-acid transporter (LAT1), readily crossing the blood-brain barrier; brain-to-blood ratios of 1.5–2 reported in long-term exposures (Suzuki et al., 1993).  
• Placenta/fetus: readily crosses placenta; fetal:maternal blood ratios 1.7–2.3 (Stern & Smith, 2003); accumulates in developing brain.  
• Other tissues: liver, kidney, hair (biomonitoring matrix), muscle. Hair:blood ratio ≈250:1, reflecting slow incorporation into keratin.  
• Demethylation: In situ conversion (mainly in liver, spleen, brain) to inorganic Hg²⁺ occurs slowly, producing even less-mobile but very slowly excreted Hg deposits (half-life in human brain → years).  

4. Elimination and persistence  
• Primary route: biliary excretion as glutathione conjugates → feces; ~1–2 % of body burden eliminated per day (WHO, 2007).  
• Enterohepatic cycling: ~20–30 % of excreted MeHg is re-absorbed, prolonging half-life.  
• Renal elimination: minor for MeHg; more relevant after demethylation to Hg²⁺.  
Persistence classification: HIGH (whole-body half-life ~2 months; brain/fetal tissue potentially >6 months).  

5. Human biomonitoring evidence (weight of evidence)  
• Fish-eating populations (Amazon, Arctic Inuit, Faroe Islands) show hair levels 2–50 µg g⁻¹ and blood levels 5–200 µg L⁻¹; these values integrate months of exposure, confirming slow clearance.  
• Autopsy studies (e.g., Magos et al., 1985) document brain Hg concentrations of 0.5–3 µg g⁻¹ in people with chronic dietary exposure, again indicating accumulation.

6. Comparison with bioaccumulation metrics used for environmental assessment  
• Fish BAF/BCF: 10⁴–10⁶ (EPA, 2013) – among the highest of any pollutant; reflects protein binding in aquatic organisms and biomagnification across food webs.  
• Although human toxicokinetic parameters differ from BCF concepts, the high dietary biomagnification means humans consume MeHg at trophic levels with substantial body burdens.

7. Toxicodynamic implications  
• Binding to neuronal tubulin/actin, perturbation of calcium homeostasis, oxidative stress, inhibition of seleno-enzymes.  
• Slow elimination prolongs exposure of sensitive tissues (developing brain, adult cerebellum, visual cortex).  
Toxicodynamic activity classification: HIGH.

8. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited quantitative data on inter-individual variability in half-life (influenced by selenium status, microbiome demethylation, polymorphisms in LAT1 or glutathione pathways).  
• Demethylation kinetics in specific tissues (especially fetal brain) not completely defined.  
• Effects of co-exposure to nutrients (e.g., long-chain ω-3 fatty acids) on distribution remain under investigation.

9. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Considering:  
– Very efficient gastrointestinal absorption (>90 %);  
– Extensive distribution to protein-rich tissues including brain and fetus;  
– Slow whole-body elimination (t₁/₂ ≈50 days) and even longer retention of inorganic Hg formed in situ;  
– Re-absorption via enterohepatic circulation;  
– Numerous biomonitoring studies showing sustained tissue levels despite episodic dietary intake;  
Methylmercury possesses a HIGH bioaccumulation potential in humans, with pronounced persistence in critical tissues such as the central nervous system and the developing fetus. Risk management therefore relies on controlling dietary intake (especially predatory fish) rather than expecting rapid clearance once exposure has occurred.

Key references  
• Clarkson TW & Magos L. Crit Rev Toxicol 2006; 36: 609-662.  
• WHO. Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury. 2007 update.  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022).  
• EPA IRIS. Methylmercury (CAS 22967-92-6) toxicological review. 2020.  
• Stern AH & Smith AE. Environ Res 2003; 92: 85-92.  
• Berglund M et al. J Toxicol Environ Health 2005; 8: 101-130.  
• Hudson RJM et al. Environ Sci Technol 1994; 28: 759-764.
