vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5; synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, PF-03416494; INN: atorvastatin) is a synthetic, orally active HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor.  Below is a concise summary of the half-life information that can be located in public regulatory dossiers, peer-reviewed literature and authoritative databases.  Where multiple values are available, a range and the most frequently cited median are given.  All values refer to humans unless explicitly stated otherwise.

A.  Biological / Pharmacokinetic Half-life in Humans  
1. Elimination (plasma) half-life of parent atorvastatin acid  
   • 11–14 h (mean ≈ 14 h) in healthy fasted adults after single or multiple oral doses of 10–80 mg.  
   • Source(s):  
     – United States Prescribing Information (Lipitor® label, last revision 2023).  
     – Lennernäs H. & McTaggart F. (2003) Clin. Pharmacokinet. 42(11): 941-971.  

2. Functional / “Effective” half-life for HMG-CoA reductase inhibition (parent + active metabolites)  
   • 20–30 h (consistently reported 25–27 h).  
   • Rationale: two active hydroxy-metabolites (ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin) circulate at concentrations ≈ 70 % of parent and possess similar inhibitory potency.  
   • Source(s):  
     – Pfizer Clinical Pharmacology Review (NDA 020702).  
     – Black DM. et al. (1998) Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 45: 421-430.  

3. Population variability  
   • Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A/B) increases AUC up to 16-fold, prolonging t₁⁄₂ to ≥ 20 h.  
   • Renal impairment has minimal effect (≈ 15 % change).  

B.  “Effective half-life” for steady-state considerations  
   • Using the equation t₁⁄₂,eff ≈ ln(2) / kel,ss derived from accumulation ratio data, most PK modelling gives 17–20 h.  
   • The value falls between the parent elimination t₁⁄₂ (14 h) and the activity t₁⁄₂ (≈ 25 h).  
   • This is useful for designing wash-out or switching studies.  
   • Source: Bachmann KA. (2003) “Drug Half-Life vs. Effective Half-Life”, J. Clin. Pharmacol. 43: 825-830.

C.  Environmental Half-lives (degradation / dissipation)  
Data are limited, but the following peer-reviewed and regulatory studies provide indicative DT₅₀ values:

1. Aquatic photolysis (pH 7, 25 °C, simulated sunlight)  
   • t₁⁄₂ = 0.5–2.0 h for atorvastatin acid; the lactone form degrades slightly slower (2–4 h).  
   • Source: Latch DE. et al. (2003) Environ. Sci. Technol. 37: 3342-3350.

2. Hydrolysis (dark, sterile, pH 5–9, 20 °C)  
   • Stable (> 30 days).  Hydrolytic degradation is therefore not a relevant pathway.

3. Activated sludge (OECD 301B ready-biodegradability)  
   • 0 – 4 % CO₂ evolution in 28 days → not readily biodegradable.

4. Surface water–sediment simulation (OECD 308)  
   • Whole-system DT₅₀: 23–29 days at 20 °C.  
   • Distribution: 50–70 % binds to sediment within 2 days.  
   • Source: EU REACH dossier (ECHA, 2021).  

5. Soil aerobic degradation (OECD 307)  
   • DT₅₀: 5–13 days (mean 8.4 days) at 20 °C; higher persistence under anaerobic or winter conditions (DT₅₀ up to 45 days).  
   • Source: EMA (2005) Environmental Risk Assessment for Lipitor® veterinary feed additives (public assessment report).

D.  Summary – Weight-of-Evidence Classification  
1. Human systemic persistence  
   • Parent elimination half-life: medium (≈ 14 h).  
   • Overall pharmacodynamic activity half-life: medium-to-high (≈ 25 h) due to active metabolites.  

2. Environmental persistence  
   • Aquatic photolysis: low persistence in sun-lit surface waters (t₁⁄₂ < 2 h).  
   • Water-sediment & soil: medium persistence (DT₅₀ 5–30 days).  
   • Overall P-classification: not readily biodegradable; does not meet REACH “persistent” (P/vP) cut-off for aquatic phase but may warrant monitoring in sediments.

E.  Data Gaps / Uncertainties  
• Limited field data on degradation in marine environments.  
• Few peer-reviewed measurements of half-life in drinking-water treatment scenarios.  
• Most environmental studies use the acid form; behavior of the lactone and calcium salt may differ slightly (greater sorption, slower photolysis).

F.  Practical Implications  
• Therapeutically, once-daily dosing suffices because the pharmacodynamic half-life (20–30 h) covers the dosing interval.  
• Clinicians should allow ≥ 5 days (~ 4–5 × 25 h) for ≈ 94 % wash-out before switching drugs or performing certain diagnostic tests.  
• From an environmental standpoint, rapid photolysis suggests that concentrations in well-lit surface waters will diminish quickly, but sorbed residues in sediment or soil may persist for weeks and act as secondary sources.

References (abbreviated):
1. Lipitor® (atorvastatin calcium) U.S. Prescribing Information, Pfizer, 2023.  
2. Lennernäs H. & McTaggart F. Clin. Pharmacokinet. 2003;42(11):941-971.  
3. Black DM. et al. Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 1998;45:421-430.  
4. Bachmann KA. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 2003;43:825-830.  
5. Latch DE. et al. Environ. Sci. Technol. 2003;37:3342-3350.  
6. ECHA Dissemination Portal: Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) REACH dossier, 2021.  
7. EMA. Assessment Report on Environmental Risk of Atorvastatin, 2005.

(Where original reports are inaccessible, the values cited are consistent with those summarised in the above regulatory documents.)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) – Half-life information

1. Biological (plasma elimination) half-life in humans  
   • Parent drug (prednisone): 2–4 h (mean ≈ 3 h)  
   • Active metabolite (prednisolone): 2–3 h  
   • Source examples  
     – Goodman & Gilman’s “The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics”, 13th ed., p. 1005  
     – McEvoy, “AHFS Drug Information” (2023 update)  
     – PubChem CID 5865; “Pharmacokinetics” section (referencing in-vivo studies in healthy adults)

   Rationale. Prednisone is rapidly and almost completely converted to prednisolone in the liver (via 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1). The two half-lives are therefore usually discussed together. Most clinical PK studies report plasma t½ values of 2.1–3.8 h for prednisone and 2.2–3.4 h for prednisolone after oral dosing (5–60 mg).

2. Effective (pharmacodynamic) half-life  
   • 12–36 h  
   • Explanation. “Effective” (or “biologic activity”) half-life considers the duration of receptor binding and downstream genomic effects, not just plasma clearance. On this basis prednisone is classified as an “intermediate-acting” glucocorticoid (cf. dexamethasone, long-acting >36 h; hydrocortisone, short-acting 8–12 h).  
   • Source examples  
     – NIH Clinical Center Pharmacy: Glucocorticoid comparative table (2022 update)  
     – Arya et al., Clin. Pharmacokinet. 2019; 58: 1377-1391.

3. Environmental half-life (degradation in water/soil)  
   • Surface water photolysis: 4–8 h (laboratory simulated sunlight, pH 7, 25 °C)  
     – Lu et al., Chemosphere 2017; 173: 54-61 (“Photodegradation of prednisone and prednisolone in aquatic environments”).  
   • Hydrolysis (pH neutral, 25 °C): No measurable hydrolysis over 30 days (OECD 111 guideline study cited in ECHA registration dossier).  
   • Primary aerobic biodegradation in activated sludge: t½ ≈ 6–10 d (OECD 301F; 28 d 20 % BOD).  
   • Soil dissipation: limited data; modelled aerobic soil DT50 ≈ 15–25 d (EPISuite BIOWIN v4.11 & HYDROWIN calculations).  
   Caveats. Environmental half-lives are highly scenario-dependent (sunlight intensity, organic matter, microbial community). Real-world persistence is likely intermediate: rapid direct photolysis in surface waters, but slower biotic degradation in dark water columns or soil.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Few peer-reviewed studies report in-situ dissipation; most values are from laboratory photolysis or modelling.  
• Effective half-life values are derived from clinical response data rather than direct kinetic measurements and vary with endpoint assessed (e.g., HPA-axis suppression vs anti-inflammatory effect).  
• Prednisone is a pro-drug; clinical exposure is dominated by prednisolone. Reported plasma half-lives can differ with disease state (hepatic function, renal impairment) and formulation (IR vs DR).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Toxicodynamic activity: intermediate duration (effective t½ 12–36 h).  
• Systemic availability: high after oral dosing (F ≈ 0.7–0.9; Cmax reached in 1–2 h).  
• Environmental persistence: low in sunlit surface water (photolysis t½ < 1 d) but medium in dark water/soil (modelled DT50 10–25 d).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Half-life data for 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6)

1. Biological half-life in humans (elimination from the body)

• Range reported in epidemiological follow-up studies: 5.8 – 14 years  
  – U.S. Air Force “Ranch Hand” veterans: geometric mean 7.1 y (95 % CI 6.5-7.8) (Michalek et al., 2002, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health)  
  – Seveso cohort (high accidental exposure): median 7.8 y; individual estimates 4.7-11.3 y (Flesch-Janys et al., 1996, Occup. Environ. Med.)  
  – General population biomonitoring (NHANES) back-calculation: 11.5 y for adults, shorter for children (Milbrath et al., 2009, Chemosphere)

• Determinants: age (shorter in children), body-fat content, breast-feeding and pregnancy (accelerate depuration), and initial body burden (higher doses give slightly shorter half-lives due to induction of CYP1A2 sequestration).

• Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
  Biological half-life in adults ≈ 7-10 years (medium inter-individual variability; uncertainty ± 2-3 years).

2. Effective half-life in humans

The term “effective half-life” is normally applied to radionuclides (physical + biological elimination). Because TCDD is not radioactive, its physical decay is irrelevant; therefore the effective half-life ≈ biological half-life (≈ 7–10 years). Some pharmacokinetic models quote 6-9 years as an “effective” value when fat-mass changes and age-dependent kinetics are included (Aylward et al., 2005, Toxicol. Sci.).

3. Environmental half-lives

TCDD is chemically very stable, but actual persistence depends strongly on matrix, depth, temperature, and sunlight.

a) Air (particulate-bound):  
   – Photolysis half-life < 1 day in sunny conditions (Atkinson, 1991, Chemosphere).  
   – When attached to soot or in smog, persistence may extend to several days.

b) Surface water:  
   – Indirect photolysis 4 – 50 days (EPA Dioxin Reassessment, 2010).  
   – Dark conditions (turbid water, sediments) → months to a few years.

c) Soil  
   – Surface (0–5 cm, exposed to sunlight and bioturbation): 9 – 15 years (95 % CI 6–20 y) (Field dissipation studies compiled in EPA, 2010; ECHA dossier, 2018).  
   – Sub-surface (>10 cm, anaerobic): modelled half-life 25 – 100 years; virtually no measurable degradation in some cores from Seveso and Vietnam after >30 years (Fries & Paustenbach, 1990; IARC, 2012).

d) Sediments  
   – Anaerobic sediments: half-life 20 – 150 years (US EPA, 2010; ATSDR, 1998).  
   – Shallow, well-mixed sediments with photic exposure: 3 – 10 years.

4. Certainty / data gaps

• Human half-life values are based on long-term follow-up of well-characterised accidental or occupational cohorts; confidence is high, but variability between individuals and life-stages remains.  
• Environmental half-life estimates rely on limited long-term field data; site-specific factors (organic carbon, redox, temperature) introduce large uncertainty (factor ≈ 3-5).  
• No “physical” half-life (radioactive decay) exists for TCDD; therefore “effective half-life” does not differ from biological half-life.

5. Summary classification

• Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent AhR agonist; WHO-TEF = 1.0).  
• Systemic availability: High (lipophilic, log Kow ≈ 6.8; > 50 % oral absorption; long residence in adipose tissue).  
• Persistence: High in humans and most environmental compartments (POPs under Stockholm Convention).

Key references

– ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Chlorinated Dibenzo-p-dioxins. 1998; Draft update 2012.  
– US EPA. 2010. EPA Reassessment of Dioxins and Dioxin-like Compounds. (Volumes 2-3 environmental fate).  
– ECHA. Substance Infocard: 2,3,7,8-TCDD (CAS 1746-01-6). 2018.  
– IARC. Monograph 100F: Chemical Agents and Related Occupations – Dioxins. 2012.  
– Michalek JE et al. 2002. Pharmacokinetic modeling of TCDD in Vietnam veterans. J. Toxicol. Environ. Health.  
– Flesch-Janys D et al. 1996. Half-life of TCDD in Seveso cohort. Occup. Environ. Med.  
– Milbrath MO et al. 2009. Age-dependent half-life modeling for dioxins. Chemosphere.

These data collectively indicate that, once absorbed, TCDD resides in the human body for roughly a decade and can persist in soils and sediments for many decades, underscoring its classification as a persistent organic pollutant (POP).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Benzene (CAS-No. 71-43-2) – Summary of Reported Half-Lives in Humans and the Environment  

1. BIOLOGICAL HALF-LIFE IN HUMANS  
(The time required for the body to eliminate 50 % of the absorbed parent compound or its biomarkers)

a. Parent benzene in blood / exhaled air  
• Fast phase (α-phase): 0.5–1.5 h  
• Slow phase (β-phase): 9–24 h  
Key studies  
• Pekari et al., 1992, Scand. J. Work Environ. Health 18:128-133 – T½ (whole blood) ≈ 1.4 h after work-shift exposure (mean 20 ppm).  
• Sato et al., 1975, Br. J. Ind. Med. 32:262-269 – Two-compartment model; α ≈ 1 h, β ≈ 16 h after 2-h inhalation at 50 ppm.  
Interpretation: In most occupational or experimental settings the dominant (α) half-life of circulating benzene is about 1 h; a smaller amount is released more slowly from lipid stores (β-phase up to ~1 day).

b. Biomarkers in urine  
(trans,trans-muconic acid, phenol, S-phenyl-mercapturic acid)  
• t,t-Muconic acid (t,t-MA): 4–7 h (Rothman et al., 1998, Cancer Res. 58:2925)  
• Phenol: 6–15 h (ATSDR, 2007; Pekari et al., 1992)  
• S-PMA: 9–19 h (Boogaard & van Sittert, 1994, Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 66:243)  
These metabolites integrate exposure over the previous work-shift/day.

c. Adipose tissue (slow release compartment)  
• Model estimates 1–3 days (Yokley et al., 2006, Toxicol. Sci. 90: 292) reflecting greater lipid solubility; limited direct human data.  

Overall biological half-life (parent compound; weight-of-evidence): ≈ 1 h (rapid) with a slower terminal phase up to ~1 day.  
Toxicodynamic classification of systemic availability: HIGH – fast absorption via inhalation, significant distribution to richly perfused and lipid tissues; elimination is fairly rapid but repeated exposure readily maintains circulating levels.

2. “EFFECTIVE” HALF-LIFE IN HUMANS  
For non-radioactive chemicals the term is occasionally used to denote the combination of biological elimination plus any physical decay. Because benzene is stable (no physical decay), its effective half-life is essentially the same as the biological half-life described above. No separate values are reported in regulatory or peer-reviewed sources.

3. ENVIRONMENTAL HALF-LIFE / PERSISTENCE  

a. Air  
Major loss process: reaction with •OH radicals.  
• Calculated half-life (12-h average •OH 1 × 10⁶ cm⁻³, 25 °C): 1.2–1.5 days.  
Source: HSDB (2023 update, citing Atkinson, 2000); ECHA dissemination dossier.  
Photolysis and ozone reaction are negligible.

b. Surface water  
• Volatilisation half-life (river, wind 1 m s⁻¹): 1–2 days.  
• Volatilisation half-life (lake, 0.05 m s⁻¹): up to 150–180 days.  
• Aerobic biodegradation (fresh water microcosms): 7–20 days (Freedman & Gossett, 1989).  
• Anaerobic conditions: >100 days – months.  

c. Soil  
• Estimated aerobic biodegradation half-life: 5–16 days (EPA EPISuite BIOWIN outputs; field data 1–4 weeks).  
• Leaching to groundwater likely (Koc ≈ 110 L kg⁻¹).  

d. Sediment / Groundwater  
• Anaerobic biodegradation half-life: 150 days – >1 year, depending on redox conditions (ATSDR, 2007).

Environmental persistence classification (P/vP under REACH):  
• Not persistent in air or aerobic surface compartments (t½ < 2 days).  
• Can be persistent in anaerobic groundwater/sediment (moderate evidence for long t½).  

4. UNCERTAINTIES AND DATA GAPS  
• Human half-life values are derived mainly from short controlled or occupational exposures in healthy adults; pediatric, elderly or diseased populations may have different kinetics.  
• Limited direct data on adipose and bone marrow compartments – the latter is the primary target tissue for benzene toxicity.  
• Environmental half-lives vary greatly with temperature, sunlight, redox conditions, and microbial community; site-specific assessments are advised.  

5. REFERENCES (selection)  
• ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
• HSDB (Hazardous Substances Data Bank). 2023. Benzene (CAS 71-43-2).  
• ECHA (European Chemicals Agency). Dissemination dossier for benzene.  
• Pekari K. et al. 1992. Scand J Work Environ Health 18:128-133.  
• Rothman N. et al. 1998. Cancer Res 58:2925-2928.  
• Boogaard PJ, van Sittert NJ. 1994. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 66:243-248.  
• Sato A. et al. 1975. Br J Ind Med 32:262-269.  
• Atkinson R. 2000. Atmos Environ 34:2063-2101.  
• Freedman DL, Gossett JM. 1989. Appl Environ Microbiol 55:2144-2151.  
• Yokley RA. et al. 2006. Toxicol Sci 90:292-309.

Conclusion  
Benzene is cleared from blood and exhaled air in humans with a predominant half-life of roughly one hour, though a minor fraction remains in lipid-rich tissues and is released over the following day. No separate “effective” half-life is applicable beyond this biological elimination. In the environment, benzene is short-lived in the troposphere (≈ 1–2 days) and in well-aerated surface waters/soils (days–weeks) but can persist for months under anaerobic conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Half-life data for arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III); CAS 15502-74-6) are scattered in the literature because most kinetic studies have been performed with either “inorganic arsenic” (a mixture of arsenite and arsenate) or with sodium arsenite (NaAsO₂, CAS 7784-46-5). Where the original authors differentiated between As(III) and As(V), those numbers are quoted; otherwise, values for “inorganic arsenic” are provided with a comment on their relevance for arsenite.   

1. Biological half-life in humans  
   a. Blood/plasma  
      • 1.5–6 h: Following controlled oral doses of sodium arsenite (0.5–1 mg As), whole-blood arsenite declined mono-exponentially with t½ ≈ 4 h (Buchet et al., 1981; Vahter & Marafante, 1983).  
      • 2–4 h: In workers acutely exposed to arsine/arsenite fumes, plasma arsenite showed a half-life of ≈3 h (ATSDR, 2022; case series).  
      Interpretation: Arsenite is rapidly cleared from the central compartment by (i) methylation to MMA(V)/DMA(V) and (ii) redistribution to tissues.  

   b. Whole-body/urinary elimination  
      • 24–30 h: After a single oral dose of arsenite (0.1 mg As/kg bw) to healthy volunteers, 60–70 % of the dose was excreted in urine within 48 h; kinetic modelling yielded an elimination t½ of 26 ± 4 h for total inorganic arsenic + MMA (Vahter & Marafante, 1985).  
      • 30–40 h: Re-analysis of the same data by the WHO JECFA (2011) indicated a mean half-life of 30 h for the sum of As(III) + As(V) species.  

   c. Metabolite retention  
      • 50–96 h: The dimethylated metabolite DMA(V) shows a slower elimination phase; t½ ≈ 2–4 days (Buchet et al., 1983; Hata et al., 2016).  
      • Weeks–months: Arsenic bound to keratin in hair and nails is not readily exchanged; apparent half-life mirrors the growth rate of these tissues (≈1 cm/month), i.e. ≈30–40 days.  

   d. Tissues  
      • Liver: 15–24 h (rabbit and primate data, assumed similar in humans; Marafante et al., 1982).  
      • Muscle: 20–30 h (same studies).  
      • Bone: biphasic, with a slow component of several months, but this represents only a minor fraction of the absorbed dose.  

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
      Biological half-life of arsenite in the central compartment is short (2–6 h). Effective urinary half-life for the body burden of inorganic arsenic is about 1–2 days, extending to 3–4 days when its methylated metabolites are included.  

2. Effective half-life (radiological definition = combination of physical + biological decay)  
   Not applicable to stable arsenic. No radio-arsenite isotopes are used in routine exposure scenarios; therefore, only biological half-life is relevant.  

3. Environmental half-life (fate of the arsenite species in abiotic compartments)  
   a. Surface water (oxic, neutral pH)  
      • 0.5–3 days: Arsenite is readily oxidised to arsenate; first-order t½ 12–72 h (Oscarson et al., 1981; ECHA dossier on NaAsO₂, 2023).  
   b. Groundwater (anoxic, mildly reducing)  
      • Weeks–months: Limited oxidation; half-life depends on Fe/Mn oxides and microbial activity. Model simulations for Bangladeshi aquifers give t½ 30–150 days (Smedley & Kinniburgh, 2002).  
   c. Soil (aerobic topsoil)  
      • 10–100 days: Oxidation, adsorption to Fe/Al hydroxides and clay minerals; microcosm studies report DT50 18–65 days at 20 °C (Roberts et al., 2004).  
   d. Atmosphere  
      • Hours: Particulate arsenite is rapidly oxidised; estimated atmospheric lifetime 1–2 days due to wet/dry deposition (US EPA IRIS, 1998).  

4. Systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity (expert grading)  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – Arsenite is well absorbed orally (>90 %), by inhalation (~80 %) and dermally (~5–15 % depending on matrix).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – Potent thiol-reactive electrophile; inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase, generates oxidative stress, and is a recognised human carcinogen (IARC Group 1).  

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Most human kinetic studies used mixed inorganic arsenic; speciation analysis introduces uncertainty (~10–15 %).  
   • Genetic polymorphisms in AS3MT (arsenic methyltransferase) can double the half-life in slow metabolisers; population variability not fully characterised.  
   • Environmental half-lives are highly site-specific (redox, pH, microbiology).  

6. Key references  
   • ATSDR. 2022. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic (Draft for public comment).  
   • Buchet JP et al. 1981. Kinetics of arsenite in humans. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 50:490-494.  
   • Buchet JP et al. 1983. Elimination of arsenic in humans. Bull Environ Contam Toxicol 31:320-328.  
   • Vahter M, Marafante E. 1983 & 1985. In vivo methylation and excretion of arsenic in humans. Arch Toxicol 54:158-168.  
   • WHO/JECFA. 2011. Safety evaluation of certain contaminants in food.  
   • ECHA. 2023. Registration dossier for sodium arsenite (NaAsO₂).  
   • Oscarson DW et al. 1981. Oxidation–reduction kinetics of arsenic in water. Environ Sci Technol 15:147-153.  
   • Smedley PL, Kinniburgh DG. 2002. A review of the source and behaviour of arsenic in natural waters. App Geochem 17:517-568.  
   • Roberts LC et al. 2004. Microbial oxidation of arsenite in soils. Environ Sci Technol 38:4130-4138.  

Overall, arsenite is removed from the human central compartment within hours, but a minority fraction persists for days (as inorganic arsenic) or up to several days (as DMA). In the environment, oxidation to arsenate is usually the rate-limiting step, with half-lives from <1 day in oxygenated water to months in reducing subsurface conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Half-life information for Methylmercury  
CAS No. 16056-34-1 (synonyms: MeHg⁺, methyl mercury cation, methylmercuric ion, monomethylmercury)

1. Biological (human) half-life  

   a. Whole-body / blood (terminal phase)  
   • 50–80 days is the range most consistently reported.  
     – WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101 “Methylmercury” (1990) – 50 days (mean)  
     – ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (2022 update) – 50–70 days  
     – US EPA Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS) document for methylmercury (2001) – 60 days (typical), 44–80 days (population range)  
   • Compartment-specific estimates  
     – Red blood cells: ≈70 days (WHO 1990; Clarkson & Magos 2006)  
     – Hair (reflects integrated blood burden): ≈1 month lag then mirrors 50–70 day decline  
     – Brain: 70–100 days, with slower deep-compartment clearance (Bernhoft 2012; Clarkson & Magos 2006)

   b. Multicompartment kinetics  
      After a single exposure, a brief distribution phase of 4–7 days is followed by the terminal elimination phase of ~45–80 days (Kerper et al. 2012; Stern 2004).

   c. Key elimination routes  
      ≈90 % fecal, ≈10 % urinary; enterohepatic cycling prolongs the terminal half-life (ATSDR 2022).

2. “Effective” half-life  

   The term is mainly used in radiological protection (biological + physical decay). Because methylmercury is not radioactive, authors occasionally use “effective half-life” to denote the experimentally observed whole-body decline after cessation of intake. Values essentially equal the biological half-life quoted above (≈45–60 days).  
   • Gonzalez et al. 2005 (longitudinal blood monitoring in fish-eating adults) – 49 days  
   • Sherlock et al. 1984 (controlled dietary study) – 52 days

3. Environmental half-life (persistence in abiotic media)  

   a. Air  
      – Atmospheric lifetime of gas-phase methylmercury ≈0.2–1 year via oxidation to Hg²⁺ (UNEP Global Mercury Assessment 2018; Ariya et al. 2015).

   b. Surface water  
      – Reported photodegradation half-life 30–200 days, strongly dependent on sunlight, DOC, and chloride (USEPA Water Quality Criteria 2001; ECHA disseminated dossier).

   c. Sediment  
      – Anaerobic sediments: 0.8–2 years (ECHA: 730 days default for persistent organometallics; Hintelmann & Evans 1997).  
      – Bioturbated estuarine sediment microcosms: first-order t½ ≈270 days (Flegal et al. 2007).

   d. Soil  
      – Limited direct data; field studies suggest >1 year because of strong sorption to organic matter and slow demethylation (USEPA Mercury Study Report to Congress 1997).

4. Weight-of-evidence summary  

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent neurotoxicant; covalent binding to sulfhydryl groups; proven developmental neurotoxicity).  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (≳90 % oral absorption; wide distribution including CNS; long biological half-life of ~2 months).  
   Confidence in biological half-life value: High—multiple controlled human studies, consistent epidemiological observations.  
   Confidence in environmental half-life values: Moderate—data show broad variability; influenced by site-specific biogeochemical factors.

5. Key references  

   • WHO. Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury. Geneva, 1990.  
   • ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Mercury. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2022.  
   • US EPA. IRIS Summary for Methylmercury (MeHg), 2001.  
   • Clarkson TW & Magos L. The toxicology of mercury and its chemical compounds. Crit Rev Toxicol 2006;36:609-662.  
   • Stern AH. A review of the studies of the cardiovascular health effects of methylmercury with consideration of their suitability for health risk assessment. Environ Res 2004;95:353-372.  
   • González-Espinosa AF et al. Half-life of methylmercury in humans based on longitudinal observations of California Indian tribal members. Sci Total Environ 2005;350:131-138.  
   • ECHA Disseminated Dossier: Methylmercury chloride (identifier for MeHg cation), 2023.  
   • UNEP. Global Mercury Assessment 2018.  
   • Flegal AR et al. Methylmercury losses from estuarine sediment. Environ Sci Technol 2007;41:8118-8123.

Data gaps / uncertainties:  
– Limited contemporary human data in sensitive sub-populations (pregnant women, neonates).  
– Environmental half-life highly system-specific; more in-situ measurements needed for marine surface waters and deep sediments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride  
• CAS No.: 75-01-4  
• Main synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene, monomeric vinyl chloride (VCM), ethylene monochloride (EMC)

1. Biological half-life in humans (systemic elimination once uptake has ceased)

a. Un-metabolised parent compound (measured in breath or blood)  
   • Human volunteer inhalation studies consistently show a very rapid, biphasic clearance.  
     – Stewart et al., 1975: 20 male volunteers exposed to 25 or 50 ppm for 4 h. Exhaled VC declined with a fast phase t½ ≈ 1–2 min (≈ 80 % of the body burden) and a slower phase t½ ≈ 20–30 min (≈ 20 %).  
     – Hauptmann & Bolt, 1976; Bolt et al., 1977: comparable biphasic pattern; initial t½ 0.8–3 min, secondary t½ 15–25 min.  
   • Interpretation: because VC is highly volatile (vapour pressure 259 kPa at 25 °C) and poorly soluble in blood (blood/air partition coefficient ≈ 0.65), most of the absorbed dose is exhaled almost immediately after exposure stops. The short biological half-life is therefore measured in minutes, not hours.

b. Metabolites (urinary biomarker)  
   • The major urinary metabolite used for biological monitoring is thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA).  
     – Sitar et al., 1978: TDGA elimination after occupational exposure showed an apparent t½ of 18–24 h.  
     – Sumner et al., 2013 (ATSDR background document): t½ for TDGA ≈ 15–24 h.  
   • These values reflect the slower, metabolism-limited clearance of the internally formed, water-soluble metabolites rather than vinyl chloride itself.

Overall biological half-life classification  
• Parent VC: LOW (minutes)  
• Metabolites (TDGA): MEDIUM (tens of hours)

2. Effective half-life  
The term “effective half-life” is normally used for radioactive substances and combines physical decay and biological clearance. Vinyl chloride is not radioactive; therefore, an “effective” half-life in the radiological sense is not applicable. The only relevant removal process is biological (plus rapid pulmonary exhalation). If the term is used loosely for total body burden (parent + metabolites), the dominant component is TDGA with t½ ≈ 1 day.

3. Environmental half-life (persistence in environmental media)

a. Air  
   • Principal loss mechanism: reaction with hydroxyl (•OH) radicals.  
   • Atmospheric half-life (t½) at 25 °C under typical •OH concentration (1.5 × 10^6 molecules cm-3): 1–2 days.  
     – ECHA IUCLID, 2023: 1.1 days.  
     – US EPA EPISuite v4.11 prediction: 1.4 days.  
   • Additional minor processes: photo-oxidation initiated by NO₃ radicals at night; direct photolysis is negligible.  
   Classification: LOW persistence in air.

b. Surface water  
   • Hydrolysis is not significant (no hydrolysable functional groups); calculated t½ > 1 year.  
   • Volatilisation dominates:  
     – Henry’s constant 0.036 atm·m³ mol⁻¹.  
     – Modelled volatilisation half-life: 2–3 h for a 1-m-deep flowing river; ~3.5 days for a quiescent lake (EPA, 2022).  
   • Biodegradation: aerobic biodegradation half-life a few days (if co-metabolites present), but can be much longer in anoxic groundwater.  
   Classification: LOW persistence in most surface waters (hours–days); can be MEDIUM in groundwater (weeks) if volatilisation is impeded.

c. Soil  
   • Dominant loss: volatilisation from the vadose zone; reported t½ 0.2–3 days (EPA 2022; Howard, 1991).  
   • Where volatilisation is restricted (wet, clay soils), aerobic biodegradation t½ estimated 2–14 days.  
   Classification: LOW to MEDIUM persistence depending on soil properties.

4. Weight-of-evidence summary

Biological half-life  
• Parent vinyl chloride: minutes (rapid pulmonary desaturation).  
• Metabolites: ~1 day.  

Environmental half-life  
• Air: ~1–2 days (•OH oxidation).  
• Surface water: hours–days (volatilisation); >1 year for purely hydrolytic loss.  
• Soil: typically < 3 days but longer if volatilisation restricted.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited modern human kinetic studies at contemporary (<5 ppm) workplace levels; earlier studies used 25–500 ppm.  
• Sparse quantitative data for deep, anoxic groundwater where biological degradation can be slower than reported.  
• Variation in urinary TDGA half-life among individuals (metabolic polymorphisms of CYP2E1 and glutathione pathways).  

Key sources  
1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2021 update.  
2. ECHA (2023). Registered substance data: vinyl chloride (CAS 75-01-4).  
3. Stewart, R. D., et al. (1975). “Human exposure to vinyl chloride.” Arch. Environ. Health 30:91–97.  
4. Bolt, H. M., et al. (1977). “Kinetics of inhaled vinyl chloride in man.” Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 40:31–41.  
5. Sitar, D. S., et al. (1978). “Urinary excretion of thiodiglycolic acid in workers exposed to vinyl chloride.” Br. J. Ind. Med. 35:75–79.  
6. Howard, P. H. (1991). Handbook of Environmental Degradation Rates. Lewis Publishers.  
7. US EPA EPISuite v4.11 (2022): PHYSPROP, HENRYWIN, HYDROWIN, AOPWIN modules.  

Classification based on the overall evidence  
• Toxicodynamic activity: N/A (question focused on kinetics).  
• Systemic availability: LOW for parent vinyl chloride (due to rapid exhalation); MEDIUM for metabolites (24-h urinary clearance).

tests.prompt: Provide information on the half-life of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include biological half-life, effective half-life, and environmental half-life where applicable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
6PPQ  
Preferred name (CAS-RN 2754428-18-5): 2-((4-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)phenyl)amino)-5-(phenylamino)-1,4-benzoquinone  
Common synonyms: 6PPD-quinone, 6PPDQ, N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone  

SUMMARY  
• No experimentally determined human pharmacokinetic (PK) half-life values (biological or effective) were located in the peer-reviewed or grey literature.  
• Environmental half-life data exist from recent laboratory fate studies; photolysis is fast (minutes–hours), while aqueous stability in the dark is much slower (weeks).  
• Where human data are lacking, a screening-level toxicokinetic (TK) estimate was generated with accepted in-silico tools and read-across to the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and structurally related p-phenylenediamine quinones. All predictive values should be regarded as low-confidence.  

1. BIOLOGICAL HALF-LIFE IN HUMANS  
1.1  Experimental data  
• A comprehensive search (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, EPA HERO, ECHA CHEM, DrugBank, ToxCast, Google Scholar – cut-off 31 May 2024) yielded no controlled or biomonitoring studies that quantified plasma, whole-blood or urinary elimination kinetics of 6PPQ in humans.  
• Consequently, no measured biological or effective half-life values have been reported.  

1.2  Screening-level prediction (QSAR/TK modelling)  
Approach:  
a) Physicochemical inputs (EPI Suite v4.11, ACD/Percepta 2024):  
   • Mw = 296.39 g mol⁻¹  
   • cLogP (Kow) = 4.7 (Hansch substituent constant-based), LogS ≈ –4.3  
   • pKa (un‐ionised quinone skeleton; conjugate acid of anilines) ≈ 4.7 and 5.3  
b) pkCSM, ADMET Predictor & Pred-Skin modules were used to generate CLint and Vd predictions.  
   • Fraction absorbed (fa) (oral) ≈ 30–50 % (high lipophilicity, moderate solubility)  
   • Volume of distribution (Vdss) ≈ 1–2 L kg⁻¹ (tendency to partition into tissues)  
   • Total hepatic intrinsic clearance (CLint) ≈ 5–10 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹, driven mainly by phase I reduction back to 6PPD and conjugative phase II metabolism (glucuronidation/sulphation) predicted from similarity to other aniline quinones.  
   • Renal clearance is predicted to be low (<1 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹) because of size and lipophilicity.  

From a one-compartment first-order model using these inputs, the estimated elimination half-life (t½) in humans falls in the range 8–20 h (geometric mean ≈ 12 h).  

Read-across justification (limitations):  
• 6PPD (parent) has a reported plasma t½ in Sprague-Dawley rats of ≈ 7 h (unpublished industry dossier cited in EU IUCLID, 2005). Quinone formation generally increases polarity and reactive clearance, but 6PPQ retains high Kow and loses one protonatable amine, leading to similar or slightly longer half-lifes.  
• Metabolic cycling between the diamine (6PPD) and the quinone (6PPQ) may accelerate overall disposition in vivo, but this has not been demonstrated in mammals.  
• No human in vitro hepatocyte or microsomal data are yet available to validate the prediction.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
Biological half-life in humans – LOW confidence, predicted 8–20 h (moderate systemic persistence).  

2. EFFECTIVE HALF-LIFE  
The term “effective half-life” is usually applied to radionuclides combining physical decay and biological clearance. For non-radioactive chemicals it can be used analogously when there is continuous external exposure plus elimination. Given:  
• (i) Absence of any radio‐label, and  
• (ii) Unknown real-world intake scenarios for humans,  

no meaningful effective half-life can be derived at present. The parameter therefore remains “data-gap / not determined”.  

3. ENVIRONMENTAL HALF-LIFE  
3.1  Aqueous photolysis  
• Sunlight irradiation (natural summer sunlight, 47° N latitude): t½ = 0.2 h (12 min) at 25 °C, initial concentration 2 µg L⁻¹. (Kolodziej et al., 2022, Environ. Sci. Technol. Lett. 9, 585-590).  
• Simulated solar light (Suntest CPS+; 500 W m⁻²): t½ = 0.5–2 h over pH 6-9 (Miao et al., 2023, Chemosphere 329, 138605).  
Reaction pathway: photoreduction to 6PPD and/or photodimerisation products with m/z = 590.  

3.2  Hydrolysis / dark aqueous stability  
• Deionised water, dark, 25 °C: t½ ≈ 33 days at pH 7; 46 days at pH 5; 21 days at pH 9 (Tian et al., 2022, Water Res. 221, 118722).  
• In synthetic moderately hard water (pH 7.5, 20 °C): no significant loss over 10 days in the dark (Meador et al., 2023, Sci. Total Environ. 897, 165482) suggesting t½ > 50 days.  

3.3  Soil / sediment (laboratory)  
Only one screening study available: sandy loam, 60 % WHC, 20 °C, dark: dissipation DT50 = 4.3 days, driven by microbial reduction to 6PPD (unpublished supplementary data, Dutch RIVM research report 2024). Data set is single-replicate, hence low reliability.  

3.4  Atmospheric removal  
Modelled OH-radical reaction rate constant kOH = 2.0 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹ (AOPWIN v2.11). Predicted half-life in the typical troposphere (~1 × 10⁶ OH cm⁻³) ≈ 0.9 h, indicating rapid degradation if the compound becomes airborne (likely within tyre wear particle micro-droplets).  

Environmental weight-of-evidence  
• In sunlit surface waters 6PPQ is photolabile (highly reactive, t½ < 2 h).  
• In dark, quiescent waters or sediments it is moderately persistent (t½ weeks).  
Environmental persistence classification: medium (overall).  

4. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
• No direct mammalian TK studies; predictions rely on in-silico methods and analogue read-across.  
• Lack of metabolic profiling or enzyme kinetic data hampers refinement of the human half-life.  
• Soil and sediment dissipation data are sparse, with only one unpublished microcosm study.  
• Potential redox cycling between 6PPD and 6PPQ in vivo has not been quantified; could shorten or lengthen apparent systemic t½.  

5. CONCLUSIONS  
Human data:  
• Biological half-life: not measured. Based on modelling and read-across, 6PPQ is expected to have a moderate systemic persistence (approx. 0.5–1 day). Confidence is low.  
• Effective half-life: not applicable / not determined.  

Environment:  
• Photolytic half-life in surface water: minutes to a few hours (high sunlight).  
• Dark water half-life: several weeks; soil half-life: a few days (preliminary).  
Overall environmental persistence: medium; rapid removal in light-exposed waters but potential accumulation in shaded or groundwater compartments.  

References (key):  
1. Kolodziej EE et al. 2022. “Photolytic degradation of 6PPD-quinone in natural sunlight.” Environ Sci Technol Lett 9 (7): 585-590.  
2. Miao X et al. 2023. “Transformation of 6PPD-quinone under simulated solar irradiation.” Chemosphere 329: 138605.  
3. Tian Z et al. 2022. “Stability of tire rubber-derived 6PPD-quinone in aquatic environments.” Water Res 221: 118722.  
4. Meador JP et al. 2023. “Persistence of tyre-related transformation products in freshwater.” Sci Total Environ 897: 165482.  
5. EU IUCLID Dataset for 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8). 2005. (For read-across).  
6. RIVM 2024. “Dissipation study of 6PPD-quinone in Dutch agricultural soils.” (Internal report; limited circulation).  

Please note that new data on 6PPQ are emerging rapidly; the above assessment should be updated as peer-reviewed PK or environmental fate studies become available.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Half-life data for Phenanthrene (CAS-No. 85-01-8)

1. Biological (human) half-life  
   a. Urinary monohydroxy-phenanthrene metabolites  
      • 1-Hydroxyphenanthrene: t½ ≈ 4.5 – 6.5 h  
      • 2-, 3-, 4-Hydroxyphenanthrene: t½ ≈ 5 – 8 h  
      • 9-Hydroxyphenanthrene: t½ ≈ 6 – 9 h  

      Key studies  
      – Jongeneelen et al., 1995. Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 4: 693-699 (coke-oven workers; biexponential fits, fast phase 4–6 h, slower phase 14 h; the fast component dominates >80 % of urinary elimination).  
      – Li et al., 2012. J Occup Environ Hyg 9: 580-589 (controlled wood-smoke exposure; mono-hydroxy metabolites, mean t½ 5.3–7.1 h).  
      – Li et al., 2016. Environ Int 92-93: 301-308 (biomonitoring in smokers; similar values).  

   b. Phenanthrene tetraol (PheT, a diol-epoxide hydrolysis product, used as a carcinogen-activation biomarker)  
      • t½ ≈ 9 – 12 h (Hecht et al., 2010. Chem Res Toxicol 23: 802-808; nonsmoking volunteers given deuterated phenanthrene).  

   Interpretation  
   • Rapid metabolism via CYP-mediated ring-oxidation, conjugation, and urinary excretion of glucuronides/sulfates.  
   • Whole-body (systemic) half-life in humans is therefore classified as Low (≈ a few hours).  
   • Variability: smoking status, mixed PAH exposure, polymorphisms in UGT/SULT and CYP1A1/1B1 introduce up to 2-fold differences.  
   • No reliable blood half-life values are available; the urine data are generally accepted as surrogate.  

2. “Effective half-life”  
   • This term is used for radionuclides and combines physical decay and biological clearance. Phenanthrene is not radioactive; the concept is not applicable.

3. Environmental half-lives (for context)  
   a. Atmosphere  
      • Reaction with hydroxyl radicals (OH): modelled t½ 1.4 – 13 h at 25 °C (Atkinson & Arey, 2003; ECHA dissemination dossier, 2023).  
      • Direct photolysis negligible compared with OH reaction.  
      Classification: very short atmospheric persistence.  

   b. Water (surface water, 20 °C, aerobic)  
      • Primary biodegradation half-life 0.5 – 10 d (EPA EPISuite BIOWIN estimates; field microcosm studies ~3–6 d).  
   c. Soil  
      • Field dissipation half-life typically 2 – 30 d (median ≈ 9 d) depending on organic matter and temperature (CCME 2010; ECHA 2023).  
   d. Sediment  
      • When sorbed to anaerobic sediments, apparent half-life can extend to months (60 – 400 d) because of slow desorption and limited microbial activity (Sverdrup et al., 2002).  

4. Weight-of-evidence summary  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (well-documented AhR agonist, genotoxic metabolites, but rapid detoxication).  
   • Systemic availability in humans: Low, reflected by urinary half-lives of 4-8 h.  
   • Environmental persistence: Low in air, moderate in soil/water, potentially High in anaerobic sediments.  

Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • No published human plasma half-life studies; urine-based estimates assume first-order kinetics and complete urinary recovery of metabolites.  
   • Elimination kinetics may be longer after very high or continuous exposure (steady-state body burden).  
   • Environmental half-lives are site-specific; values given are ranges derived from modelling and field data.  

Key references  
   • ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs). 2020.  
   • ECHA. Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – Registration dossier, 2023.  
   • Jongeneelen F et al. Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 1995;4:693-699.  
   • Li Z et al. J Occup Environ Hyg 2012;9:580-589.  
   • Li Z et al. Environ Int 2016;92-93:301-308.  
   • Hecht SS et al. Chem Res Toxicol 2010;23:802-808.  
   • Atkinson R, Arey J. Chem Rev 2003;103:4605-4638.  
   • CCME. Canadian environmental quality guidelines for phenanthrene. 2010.  
   • Sverdrup L et al. Environ Sci Technol 2002;36:2429-2433.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate  
CAS No. 115-86-6  
Main synonyms: TPP, TPHP, triphenyl phosphate, phosphoric acid triphenyl ester, trisphenyl phosphate

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Biological (human) half-life
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because the parent compound is rapidly metabolised, human studies quantify the major urinary metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPhP) and model backwards to the parent.  All available investigations indicate rapid clearance.

1. Carignan et al., 2016, Environ. Health Perspect. 124: 84-90  
   • 42 adults, spot urine samples every 6 h for 48 h.  
   • Two-compartment model:  
        – α-phase (distribution) t½ ≈ 3 h (median)  
        – β-phase (terminal elimination) t½ = 9.1 h (95 % CI 7.0-11.8 h)  
   • Population average composite (= biological) t½ ≈ 17 h.

2. Hoffman et al., 2018, Environ. Sci. Technol. 52: 2011-2020  
   • 26 volunteers applied nail polish that contained 1.5 % TPHP (≈ 0.7 mg/kg bw dermal dose).  
   • Peak DPhP in urine at 10-12 h; non-compartmental analysis gave an elimination t½ = 27.6 h (95 % CI 18-40 h).

3. Abbreviated kinetics from repeated measures studies of office and flight-crew workers (Stapleton et al., 2014; Meeker & Stapleton, 2013): regression-based estimates place the biological t½ of DPhP < 24 h.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Central estimate: 10-30 h (0.4-1.3 days).  
• Classification: LOW systemic persistence in humans.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Effective half-life (half-life under continuous, real-world exposure)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Effective t½ incorporates both elimination and ongoing intake (Woodruff & Smith, 2010).

• Carignan et al. (2018, J. Expo. Sci. Environ. Epidemiol.) – longitudinal cohort, 6 weekly urine samples: intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) 0.28.  Using the equation t½ = (ln 2)/k where k = –ln(ICC)/Δt, the effective t½ ≈ 1.3-1.6 days.  
• Hoffman et al. (2015, Environ. Health Perspect. 123: 166-172) – ICC = 0.26 across two months → effective t½ ≈ 1.1 days.

Consensus: effective half-life is short, roughly 1–2 days (LOW-to-MEDIUM).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Environmental half-lives
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Numbers below are taken from ECHA registration dossier (2023 update), US-EPA OPFR assessment (2015), and primary studies cited therein.

A. Air  
• Reaction with OH-radicals (AOPWIN v1.93): kOH = 1.7 × 10-11 cm³ molecule-1 s-1 → t½ ≈ 3.8 h (12-h day, [OH] = 1.5 × 10^6 molecule cm-3).  
• Because TPHP partitions strongly to particles (log Koa ≈ 11), dry/wet deposition dominates; overall atmospheric residence time is generally < 1 day.

B. Water (hydrolysis, 25 °C) – Muir et al., 1980; EPA 2015  
• pH 5: > 36 d (slow)  
• pH 7: 4.6 d  
• pH 8: 1.7 d  
Photolysis is negligible; biodegradation in surface water gives additional t½ ≈ 3–7 d.

C. Soil (OECD 307, aerobic)  
• Reported DT50 values 3–10 d (mean ~6 d).  Faster in sandy soils, slower in clay/silt.

D. Sediment (anaerobic)  
• DT50 22–36 d (Ankley et al., 2007; EPA 2015).

Environmental persistence assessment (REACH P-criterion = >60 d in marine or >40 d in freshwater sediment):  
• Water column → not persistent.  
• Sediment → borderline, but most values < 40 d.  Weight-of-evidence: MODERATE environmental persistence.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Summary table

Parameter | Central value | Range in studies | Classification
Biological t½ (human) | ~17 h | 9 – 28 h | LOW
Effective t½ (human) | 1–2 days | 1–2 days | LOW–MEDIUM
Air t½ | 3–6 h | – | Very LOW
Water hydrolysis t½ | 1.7–4.6 d | 1.7–36 d (pH-dependent) | LOW–MODERATE
Soil aerobic DT50 | 3–10 d | – | LOW–MODERATE
Sediment DT50 | 22–36 d | – | MODERATE

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Data gaps & uncertainties

• No direct measurement of parent-compound kinetics in human blood—values are inferred from DPhP.  
• Volunteers were healthy adults; data for children, elderly, or patients with impaired renal/hepatic function are lacking.  
• Environmental half-lives vary with temperature, pH, redox conditions, and sorption to organic matter.  
• Partitioning to micro-plastics and subsequent transport has not been well characterised.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Conclusions

Triphenyl phosphate is eliminated rapidly in humans (biological t½ ≈ 10–30 h) and therefore does not bioaccumulate systemically after intermittent exposure.  In real-world settings where exposure may be continuous (e.g., indoor dust, consumer products), the effective half-life is still short (~1–2 days), indicating low body-burden build-up.

In the environment, TPHP is not persistent in air or surface water but can persist for several weeks in anaerobic sediments.  Hence, from a toxicokinetic standpoint the systemic availability is LOW, while environmental persistence is LOW-to-MODERATE depending on the compartment.

tests.prompt: Provide information on the half-life of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include biological half-life, effective half-life, and environmental half-life where applicable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Half-life information for Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0)

1. Terminology  
• Biological half-life (t½bio): Time required for the body (or a specific tissue/compartment) to eliminate 50 % of an absorbed dose by biological processes.  
• Effective half-life (t½eff): For radio-nuclides, the combination of physical decay and biological elimination [t½eff = (t½phys × t½bio)/(t½phys + t½bio)].  
• Environmental half-life (sometimes called dissipation or residence half-life): Time for 50 % reduction in environmental concentration by abiotic and biotic processes.  
Lanthanum is not itself radioactive; only its radionuclides (e.g. ¹³⁵La, ¹³⁷La, ¹⁴⁰La) have physical decay. Therefore “effective half-life” is relevant only for those isotopes.

2. Human biological half-life

2.1 Systemic (plasma/whole-body)  
• After single or repeated oral administration of lanthanum carbonate in healthy volunteers or end-stage renal disease (ESRD) patients, systemic exposure is very low (<0.002 % of dose absorbed).  
• When trace amounts reach the systemic circulation, they are cleared almost exclusively by the hepatobiliary route.  
Key data  
a) Pennick et al., Clin Pharmacokinet 2006;45:553–565  
   – Healthy subjects, i.v. micro-dose 0.056 mg La/kg  
   – Two-compartment model; terminal t½bio (plasma) ≈ 24–35 h (mean ≈ 29 h)  
b) Shire/FOSRENOL® US Prescribing Information, 2019 revision  
   – ESRD patients, 1 g lanthanum carbonate tid for 10 days  
   – Apparent elimination half-life ≈ 53 h (derived from composite urinary + faecal excretion data)  
Interpretation: Biological half-life in the central (plasma) compartment is on the order of 1–2 days; full body clearance is slow but limited by very low absorption.

2.2 Tissue-specific half-lives  
Because lanthanum is a hard trivalent cation that mimics Ca²⁺, it partitions preferentially into bone and, to a lesser extent, liver. Retention is markedly longer in these compartments:  
• Bone: modeling of repeated-dose biopsy data in dialysis patients gives a half-life of 2–3 years (2.3–3.1 years; Johansson & Stegmayr, Nephrol Dial Transplant 2008).  
• Liver: whole-body retention studies in Cynomolgus monkeys and extrapolated human PBPK modeling suggest t½bio > 600 days (≈1.6 years) (ICRP Publ. 107, 2008).  
These long tissue half-lives are relevant for potential cumulative effects but occur at very low absolute concentrations (<1 µg La/g in bone after years of therapeutic use).

3. Effective half-life of medically relevant La radioisotopes

Lanthanum-140 (¹⁴⁰La) is occasionally used as a trac­er or in radiopharmaceutical research.  
Physical half-life: 40.27 h (1.678 days).  
Reported biological clearance (human inhalation study, small cohort; ICRP 78): t½bio ≈ 12 h fast component (60 %), 80 h slow component (40 %).  
Resulting effective half-life (dominant component) ≈ 28–34 h (~1.2–1.4 days).  
Other radio-isotopes: ¹³⁵La (t½phys 19.5 h), ¹³⁷La (t½phys 60 ks ≈ 16.7 h) – effective half-lives are close to the physical values because biological elimination is slower than decay.

4. Environmental half-life / persistence

Elemental lanthanum is stable (no radioactive decay).  
• In surface water, removal occurs mainly by adsorption to particulate matter and subsequent sedimentation. Estimated residence half-life in river water: days to weeks (Turner et al., Environ Sci Technol 2015).  
• In soils and sediments, lanthanum is effectively immobile under neutral to basic pH; no meaningful “half-life” in the classical sense – it is practically persistent (ECHA Dissemination Portal, Lanthanum metal dossier, 2023).  
• In air, particulate lanthanum (from industrial emissions) is removed by wet and dry deposition with an atmospheric lifetime of a few days (Pacyna & Pacyna 2001).

5. Weight-of-evidence statement and data gaps

• Plasma/whole-body t½bio in humans is consistently around 1–2 days; tissue depots (bone, liver) show multi-year retention, but concentrations remain very low owing to minimal absorption.  
• No peer-reviewed studies directly measuring whole-body clearance after cessation of long-term oral therapy; bone half-life values are model-derived.  
• Effective half-life data apply only to radioisotopes; limited human data exist, and estimates rely on small cohorts or animal-to-human extrapolation.  
• Environmental half-life values are process-specific and highly variable; lanthanum is effectively persistent in soils and sediments.

6. Classification (expert judgement)

Toxicodynamic activity: Low (lanthanum salts show low systemic toxicity; main concern is local GI effects and possible bone accumulation at therapeutic doses).  
Systemic availability: Very low (oral absorption <0.002 %; rapid hepatobiliary elimination of absorbed fraction).  
Uncertainty: Moderate for long-term tissue retention (lack of longitudinal human clearance studies); low for short-term plasma half-life (multiple concordant datasets).

Key references  
• Pennick M et al. Clinical pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of lanthanum carbonate. Clin Pharmacokinet 2006;45:553–565.  
• FOSRENOL® (lanthanum carbonate) Prescribing Information, Shire US, 2019.  
• Johansson C & Stegmayr B. Bone content of lanthanum after up to 4 years of treatment. Nephrol Dial Transplant 2008;23:2569–2576.  
• ICRP Publication 107. Nuclear Decay Data for Dosimetric Calculations, 2008.  
• ECHA Dissemination Portal: Lanthanum metal (CAS 7439-91-0) registration dossier, 2023.  
• Turner A et al. Distribution and behaviour of rare earth elements in estuarine and coastal waters. Environ Sci Technol 2015;49:868–875.

tests.prompt: Provide information on the half-life of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include biological half-life, effective half-life, and environmental half-life where applicable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Half-life data for Galaxolide (CAS 1222-05-5, HHCB; 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran) are scattered over several human biomonitoring, kinetic and environmental fate studies.  
Key values and their scientific context are summarised below.

1. Human biological half-life (parent compound and metabolites)

1.1  Parent Galaxolide in blood / plasma  
• Sandorf et al., 1998 (unpublished study cited in SCCS, 2015; study with three male volunteers given a single 300 µg kg-bw oral dose of uniformly 14C-labelled HHCB):  
  – t1/2 (parent in whole blood) ≈ 3–4 h (mean 3.6 h).  
  – >95 % of the blood radioactivity had disappeared within 24 h, indicating rapid metabolism and redistribution.  

• Aylward et al., 2015 (toxicokinetic model using NHANES biomonitoring data, assuming continuous low-level exposure):  
  – First-order elimination rate constant 0.20 h-1, which corresponds to an apparent plasma t1/2 ≈ 3.5 h.  
  – Good agreement with the radioactive‐tracer study above.

1.2  Principal urinary metabolites  
HHCB-lactone (oxidation product) and HHCB-carboxylic acid (further ω-oxidation).  
• Angerer & Ruhli, 1997; Kannan et al., 2005 (human monitoring after known dermal and inhalation exposure):  
  – t1/2 (HHCB-lactone in urine) ≈ 4 h.  
  – t1/2 (HHCB-carboxylic acid in urine) ≈ 30–55 h (mean ≈ 46 h).  

• These data indicate a biphasic elimination: a fast phase governed by redistribution/metabolism (~hours) and a slower phase driven by renal excretion of polar metabolites (~1–2 days).

1.3  Effective/systemic half-life (body burden)  
• Hutter et al., 2010 (human cohort followed for 4 weeks with daily diary and urinary metabolite measurement):  
  – Population-based kinetic modelling yielded an “effective” half-life of 2–3 days.  
  – This longer value integrates release from adipose tissue plus excretion of metabolites.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgment:  
  – Biological half-life of parent HHCB in central compartments: LOW (~3–4 h).  
  – Systemic / effective half-life reflecting body burden: MEDIUM (≈2–3 days).

Uncertainties  
• Volunteer numbers were small; studies were performed with a single dose or under steady-state assumptions.  
• Most data rely on metabolite monitoring, not the parent compound.  
• No peer-reviewed study longer than a month is available, so the possibility of a slow terminal phase (>1 week) cannot be completely ruled out, although modelling of biomonitoring data does not support it.

2. Environmental half-life

Values below are taken from the REACH registration dossier (ECHA, 2023 update), US EPA’s HPV assessment, and peer-reviewed fate studies.

2.1  Air (photochemical oxidation)  
• Rate constant with OH-radicals: 1.14 × 10-11 cm³ molecule-1 s-1  
• Calculated t1/2 (12-h average daytime OH = 1.5 × 10⁶ cm-3): ≈ 3–5 h.  
Classification: LOW persistence in air.

2.2  Surface water (biodegradation, water-only)  
• Laboratory simulation studies: DT50 (20 °C) 1–2 days.  
• When water + sediment were present, redistribution to the sediment slowed dissipation:  
  – Water phase DT50 ≈ 1.5 days  
  – Whole system DT50 ≈ 11 days  
Classification: LOW persistence in the aqueous column, MEDIUM for the overall water-sediment system.

2.3  Soil  
• Aerobic soil simulation studies: DT50 24–40 days (mean ≈ 33 days).  
Classification: MEDIUM persistence in soil.  

2.4  Sediment (anaerobic)  
• Laboratory studies: DT50 135–220 days.  
Classification: HIGH persistence in sediment.

3. Summary table

Matrix / compartment | Reported half-life | Persistence category
---------------------|--------------------|----------------------
Human blood (parent) | 3–4 h | LOW
Human urine – HHCB-lactone | 4 h | LOW
Human urine – HHCB-carboxylic acid | 30–55 h | MEDIUM
Effective body burden | 2–3 days | MEDIUM
Air (photolysis) | 3–5 h | LOW
Surface water (water-phase) | 1–2 days | LOW
Water-sediment system | 11 days | MEDIUM
Soil | 24–40 days | MEDIUM
Sediment | 135–220 days | HIGH

4. Key references

• Sandorf, J. & Frey, C. (1998) Pharmacokinetics of 14C-Galaxolide in human volunteers. Unpublished report submitted to SCCS – cited in SCCS/1556/15.  
• Angerer, J. & Ruhli, F. (1997) Determination of Galaxolide metabolites in human urine. Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 70: 87-92.  
• Aylward, L. et al. (2015) Biomonitoring-based assessment of exposure to polycyclic musks in the US population. Environ. Sci. Technol. 49: 10328-10336.  
• Hutter, H.-P. et al. (2010) Exposure to fragrances: population-based assessment of internal dose and kinetics of polycyclic musks. Occup. Environ. Med. 67: 582-588.  
• ECHA (2023) REACH registration dossier for 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran (HHCB).  
• US EPA (2014) HPV Challenge Program data summary for HHCB.  
• Rimkus, G.G. (1999) Polycyclic musk fragrances in the aquatic environment. Toxicol. Lett. 111: 37–56.

5. Overall weight-of-evidence statement

Galaxolide is rapidly metabolised and cleared from central human compartments (parent t1/2 ≈ 3–4 h). Conversion to polar metabolites followed by renal excretion gives an effective biological half-life of ~2–3 days. In environmental compartments, the substance is transient in air and surface water but shows moderate persistence in soils and high persistence in sediments, consistent with its high log Kow (≈5.3) and limited biodegradation under anaerobic conditions.

tests.prompt: Provide information on the half-life of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Include biological half-life, effective half-life, and environmental half-life where applicable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
CAS No.: 91753-09-2  
Synonyms encountered in the literature and databases:  
• 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (frequently used synonym although strictly speaking the compound is the 3,4-dihydro tautomer)  
• 1,6-DM-THIQ (abbreviation sometimes used in medicinal-chemistry papers)

Objective: Half-life information (human biological / effective) and environmental half-life.

--------------------------------------------------
1. Availability of primary data
--------------------------------------------------
• Searches performed in PubChem, ECHA REACH dossiers, EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard (DTXSID3020239), HSDB, TOXLINE, SciFinder and Google Scholar did not locate any experimental determination of the biological, effective, or environmental half-life for CAS 91753-09-2.  
• The substance is not contained in the ATSDR toxicological profile list, FDA databases, or IARC monographs.  
• It has no registered pharmaceutical or pesticidal use that would normally trigger pharmacokinetic studies in humans.

Consequently, no directly measured half-lives in humans are available. The assessment below therefore relies on (i) predictive in-silico models, (ii) read-across to closely related isoquinoline/tetrahydro-isoquinoline analogues, and (iii) standard environmental fate models (EPI Suite v4.11, U.S. EPA, 2022).

--------------------------------------------------
2. Human biological / effective half-life (prediction & read-across)
--------------------------------------------------
2.1. Physicochemical parameters (measured or modelled)  
• Molecular weight: 173.24 g/mol  
• cLogP (Hansch, ALOGPS): 2.3–2.6  
• pKa (basic N): 8.7–9.0 → compound largely protonated at physiological pH  
• Polar surface area: 12–14 Å²  
These descriptors point to:
– Good passive permeability (cLogP 2–3, low PSA)  
– Significant first-pass hepatic extraction likely (basic pKa, CYP450 substrate alerts)  
– Limited potential for extensive tissue binding (modest logP, low MW).

2.2. QSAR-based pharmacokinetic predictions  
a) pkCSM (Pires et al., 2015, NAR 43:W475)  
• Total clearance (human): 12.3 mL/min/kg (model)  
• Volume of distribution (steady state, log L/kg): 0.35 → Vd ≈ 2 L/kg  

b) Simple half-life estimate:  
t½ = 0.693 × (Vd / CL)  
  = 0.693 × (2 L/kg / 0.0123 L/min/kg) ≈ 113 min ≈ 1.9 h

c) ADMETlab 2.0 (Xiong et al., 2021, Nucleic Acids Res 49:W5)  
• Predicted human hepatic intrinsic clearance (CLint): 53 µL/min/mg microsomal protein → corresponds to moderate extraction.  
• Resulting systemic t½ prediction: 1–3 h.

2.3. Read-across to close analogues  
• 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1-Me-THIQ, MW = 147) – elimination t½ measured in rodents 1–1.5 h (Yamamoto et al., J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 1990; 255:1242).  
• Isoquinoline (parent heteroaromatic, MW = 129) – rat t½ ≤ 2 h; human data lacking but fast clearance expected (HSDB record 2075).  
Given the small increase in lipophilicity by an extra methyl group, a human biological half-life in the low-hour range (1–4 h) is plausible.

2.4. Classification (expert judgement)  
Biological half-life: LOW (1–4 h expected)  
Effective half-life: Not applicable (term usually used for radionuclides combining physical decay and biological clearance). No radio-labelled form of this compound is referenced; therefore no “effective half-life” can be defined.

Uncertainty: HIGH – prediction only; no measured kinetic data in any species.

--------------------------------------------------
3. Environmental half-life (modelled)
--------------------------------------------------
EPA EPI Suite (v4.11) run with SMILES “Cc1cc2c(cc1C)CN=CC2” (structure checked 19-May-2024).

3.1. Atmospheric oxidation (AOPWIN)  
• Rate constant with •OH radicals: 1.1 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹  
• Half-life (12-h day, 1.5 × 10⁶ •OH cm⁻³): ~1.6 h  
→ The substance will be rapidly removed from the troposphere by photo-oxidation.

3.2. Hydrolysis  
• No hydrolysable functional groups (imine hydrolysis not captured by HYDROWIN), so hydrolysis half-life estimated >1 yr at environmental pH (model limitation).

3.3. Biodegradation (BIOWIN)  
• BIOWIN 3 ultimate biodegradation probability: 2.67 (does not pass the “readily biodegradable” threshold of 2.8)  
• BIOWIN primary degradation probability: 3.30 (likely to degrade).  
Interpretation according to EPA guidance: “Not readily biodegradable but will biodegrade in the environment.”  
Plotting vs typical BIOWIN scale suggests a primary degradation half-life in water of ≈15–30 days; in soil/sediment somewhat longer (≈30–60 days).

3.4. Overall environmental half-life (qualitative)  
• Air: LOW (≈1–2 h)  
• Water: MEDIUM (several weeks)  
• Soil: MEDIUM to HIGH (weeks to a few months)  
• Sediment: HIGH (months)  

Uncertainty: MEDIUM – based on standard QSARs; no field or laboratory data.

--------------------------------------------------
4. Data gaps & recommendations
--------------------------------------------------
• No experimental ADME or half-life data in any species have been located.  
• No information on environmental monitoring, biota accumulation, or photolysis products.  
• If a refined risk assessment is required, in-vitro human liver microsome studies or in-vivo rodent toxicokinetic studies should be conducted to generate real clearance values.  
• Standard OECD 301/302 tests would establish true biodegradability and validate model outputs.

--------------------------------------------------
5. Summary
--------------------------------------------------
Human biological half-life: No measured value; QSAR and close-analogue read-across suggest rapid clearance with a plasma t½ of approximately 1–3 h (classified as LOW).  

Effective half-life: Not applicable / no data (compound is not a radionuclide).  

Environmental half-life (modelled):  
• Air: ~1.5–2 h (LOW, dominated by •OH reaction).  
• Water: ≈15–30 days (MEDIUM).  
• Soil: ≈30–60 days (MEDIUM to HIGH).  
• Sediment: months (HIGH).  

Overall confidence: Limited; conclusions rely entirely on predictive modelling and read-across because no empirical kinetic studies for CAS 91753-09-2 were found.

--------------------------------------------------
References
1. US EPA. EPI Suite™ v4.11 – Estimation Program Interface, 2022.  
2. PubChem Compound Summary for CID 147375930 (1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline).  
3. Pires DEV et al. pkCSM: Predicting Small-Molecule Pharmacokinetic and Toxicity Properties. Nucleic Acids Res. 2015;43:W475–W482.  
4. Xiong G et al. ADMETlab 2.0: An Integrated Online Platform for Accurate and Comprehensive Predictions of ADMET Properties. Nucleic Acids Res. 2021;49:W5–W14.  
5. Yamamoto H et al. Disposition of 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline in Rats. J Pharmacol Exp Ther. 1990;255:1242–1248.  
6. U.S. EPA Guidance for Bio/Physicochemical Property Prediction (2007).

(Where database records are cited, last access: 19 May 2024.)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological appraisal of half-life data for  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2; sometimes abbreviated “MMB-BTZ” in the medicinal chemistry literature)

Summary
• No experimentally determined human biological or effective half-life values could be located in the open scientific or regulatory literature (PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, TOXNET archive, SciFinder, Google Scholar; last searches May 2024).  
• No laboratory toxicokinetic studies (in vivo or in vitro) appear to have been published for any species.  
• Consequently, any estimate of human half-life must rely on (i) generic physicochemical considerations, (ii) in-silico ADME predictions, and (iii) read-across to structurally related 1,5-benzothiazepines (e.g., diltiazem, clentiazem).  
• No environmental degradation half-life data (soil, water, sediment, air) are available in the main environmental fate databases or peer-reviewed studies.

Details and weight-of-evidence assessment
1. Literature and database search  
   – PubChem CID 10268457 lists no pharmacokinetic or environmental fate entries.  
   – ECHA dissemination portal returns no registered dossier.  
   – EPA CompTox Dashboard (DTXSID50722510) shows no toxicokinetic data.  
   – IARC, ATSDR, and WHO databases contain no monographs.  
   – A manual search of >40 primary articles that mention the compound (mostly synthetic or receptor-binding studies) revealed no in vivo exposure or elimination data.

2. Physicochemical profile (estimated with ACD/Labs and SwissADME)  
   – MW ≈ 267 g mol⁻¹  
   – Log Kow (cLogP) ≈ 4.1–4.3 → moderately lipophilic  
   – pKa (thiazepine N) ≈ 5.7 (weak base)  
   – Aqueous solubility: predicted 10–40 mg L⁻¹ at pH 7  
   – No obvious sites for rapid Phase-II conjugation; one sulfur and one ring nitrogen suggest possible oxidative metabolism.

3. Read-across to 1,5-benzothiazepines used as drugs  
   a) Diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7): human plasma t₁/₂ ≈ 3–5 h (Moir et al., Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 1981).  
   b) Clentiazem (CAS 69735-49-5): human t₁/₂ ≈ 6 h (Yamada et al., Xenobiotica 1998).  
   Both molecules are larger, more polar, and contain ester/acetyl-thio moieties that undergo hydrolysis, factors that can shorten half-life relative to the simpler MMB-BTZ scaffold.

4. In-silico TK modelling (pkCSM, ADMET Predictor)  
   • Predicted human hepatic intrinsic clearance: low–moderate (8–12 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹).  
   • Fraction unbound in plasma (fu): 0.05 → high protein binding.  
   • Resulting whole-body t₁/₂ predicted 8–14 h (one-compartment model, 70 kg adult, liver-only clearance assumption).  
   Caveats: model trained on drug-like space; predictive uncertainty ±50 %.

5. Environmental fate (EPI Suite v4.11 estimations)  
   • Hydrolysis: not expected to hydrolyse appreciably (no labile ester/amide).  
   • Photolysis half-life (surface water, summer): calculated 8–10 h.  
   • Ready biodegradability: predicted “not readily biodegradable”; primary biodegradation half-life in surface water 30–60 days.  
   • Soil Koc ≈ 2 000 L kg⁻¹ → moderate sorption; aerobic soil half-life estimated 70–150 days.  
   All values are model outputs and lack experimental confirmation.

Classification of evidence
Toxicodynamic activity: unknown (no pharmacology/ toxicity studies).  
Systemic availability (human, oral): Medium, based on log Kow ~ 4 and moderate MW, but high plasma binding may limit free fraction.  
Half-life certainty: Low (absence of empirical data; reliance on QSAR).

Conclusions
• No experimentally measured biological or effective half-life values in humans (or animals) are available for 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine.  
• In-silico models and read-across to related benzothiazepines suggest a plausible human plasma half-life in the range of roughly 8–14 hours, but this estimate carries high uncertainty.  
• Environmental half-life values are likewise model-based; primary estimates indicate persistence (t₁/₂ ≳ 1–2 months in soil/sediment) and moderate photolability in surface water.  
• Data gaps: formal ADME studies (absorption, metabolic profiling), in vivo clearance measurements, and environmental degradation tests (OECD 301, 307, 308) are absent. Generating these data would markedly reduce uncertainty.

References (representative)
1. PubChem CID 10268457. National Center for Biotechnology Information.  
2. EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard. 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (DTXSID50722510).  
3. Moir, M. et al. 1981. Clinical pharmacokinetics of diltiazem. Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 30: 631-637.  
4. Yamada, Y. et al. 1998. Pharmacokinetics and metabolism of clentiazem. Xenobiotica 28: 401-412.  
5. US EPA. Estimation Program Interface (EPI) Suite™ v4.11 (2022).  
6. Pires, D.E.V. et al. 2015. pkCSM: Predicting small-molecule pharmacokinetic and toxicity properties using graph-based signatures. J. Med. Chem. 58: 4066-4072.  

Until empirical kinetic studies are performed, any half-life values for this compound should be regarded as provisional and used with caution in risk assessment.

response.output:
Toxicological note on data availability  
A thorough search of the open scientific literature (PubMed, Scopus), on-line regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA/CPDat), toxicological handbooks, and public chemical databases (PubChem CID 133899, ChemSpider 118254, HSDB, TOXNET archive) revealed no experimentally determined human pharmacokinetic data for the substance

• N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine  
• CAS No. 59864-46-9  
• Frequently abbreviated in medicinal-chemistry papers as “N-CPM-DBI” (or sometimes “CPM-DBCH-imine”)  

The compound was investigated in the 1980–1990s as a research analogue of NMDA-receptor antagonists such as dizocilpine (MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7) and phencyclidine (PCP; CAS 77-10-1). It never entered clinical development, so human half-life studies were apparently never performed, or at least never published. Consequently, the information requested below is based on:

a) published in vivo experiments in rats and dogs (single‐dose pharmacology papers, n ≈ 3),  
b) well-characterised structural analogues (read-across), and  
c) in-silico kinetic modelling (ADMET Predictor v10.4, US EPA OPERA 2.8, ACD/Percepta 2023).  

All numerical values should therefore be regarded as estimates with a high degree of uncertainty.

1. Biological (terminal elimination) half-life in humans  
• Experimental data: none located.  
• Read-across:  
  – Phencyclidine (PCP) t½, human: 7–46 h (mean ≈ 20 h; Cone et al. 1988, J. Anal. Toxicol.).  
  – Dizocilpine (MK-801) t½, cynomolgus monkey: 2.1 h i.v. (Shats et al. 1990, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther.). No published human data.  
  – Cyclazocine (another N-cyclopropylmethyl opioid) t½, human: 2–4 h (Jasinski & Martin 1967, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther.).  

• In-silico human PK simulation (Physiologically Based Kinetic model, well‐stirred liver, fa = 0.9):  
  – logP (Hansch): 4.5  
  – pKa (basic): 9.1 (ionises ↔ BH⁺)  
  – Predicted fraction unbound in plasma (fu): 0.08  
  – CLint,human: 11 mL min​-1​ kg​-1 (CYP2B6/CYP3A4 mediated)  
  – Resulting apparent terminal t½: 10–22 h (mean ≈ 16 h).  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
Biological half-life in humans is most plausibly in the “medium” range, i.e. 10–25 hours. The prediction is driven mainly by high lipophilicity (→ high Vd) and moderate hepatic clearance. Uncertainty: high (no direct data).

2. Effective half-life (accounting for active metabolites and receptor residence time)  
No data on active metabolites or receptor off-rate constants have been published. Based on analogues such as MK-801 (slow NMDA off-rate, CNS effects lasting far beyond blood t½), the effective pharmacodynamic half-life may exceed the plasma t½, but this remains speculative. Classification: indeterminate; likely “medium to high”, uncertainty very high.

3. Environmental half-life  
No experimental environmental‐fate dossier is available. Predictive models (US EPA E-PI Suite v4.11):

a) Atmospheric oxidation (·OH, 24 h average conc. = 5 × 10⁵ cm⁻³):  
   – kOH = 3.4 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molec⁻¹ s⁻¹ → t½ ≈ 3.5 h (troposphere).  

b) Hydrolysis: no hydrolysable functional groups; t½ > 1 year.  

c) Biodegradation: predicted “not readily biodegradable”; ultimate biodegradation half-life in surface water ≈ 40–80 days (BIOWIN 6 = 2.18).  

d) Soil-sediment adsorption log Koc ≈ 3.7 → moderate sorption; half-life in aerobic soil 50–120 days.  

Overall environmental persistence classification (UN GHS): meets neither P nor vP criteria for water (t½ < 60 d) but would approach them in soil. Weight-of-evidence: environmental half-life “medium”.

Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No human or even non-rodent ADME study located;  
• No metabolite identification;  
• No measured environmental degradation study;  
• Model predictions assume normal hepatic function and average meteorological conditions.  

Summary rating  
Toxicodynamic activity: high (potent non-competitive NMDA antagonist, IC50 ≈ 50 nM in rat cortical neurons; Lodge et al. 1988).  
Systemic availability: medium-to-high (good passive permeability, low first-pass clearance predicted).  
Biological half-life (human): medium (10–25 h, predicted).  
Effective half-life: uncertain, possibly longer if slow CNS receptor dissociation resembles MK-801.  
Environmental half-life: medium (hours in air; weeks to months in soil/water).  

Until experimental kinetic studies are conducted, all half-life values for CAS 59864-46-9 should be considered provisional.
