vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological query: biological, effective, and environmental half-life data for  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)

1. Data search strategy  
   • Primary databases consulted (May 2024): PubChem, ECHA dossiers, EPA CompTox, HSDB, TOXLINE/NIH, Scifinder, Web of Science, Google Scholar.  
   • Secondary resources: EPISuite v4.11 (US-EPA) for environmental fate modelling; pkCSM and ADMETlab 2.0 for in-silico human PK prediction.  
   • Search terms: “4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline”, “CAS 29248-42-8”, “half-life”, “clearance”, “persistence”, “ADME”, “t1/2”.

2. Outcome of literature and dossier search  
   • No experimental kinetic studies in humans, animals, or environmentally relevant media were located.  
   • Substance is not registered under EU-REACH and is absent from HSDB; therefore no dossier-quality kinetic data exist.  
   • Only limited physico-chemical information (logKow, water solubility, vapour pressure) is available in PubChem (CID 12719679).  
   • Consequently, half-life values must be estimated from closely related isoquinoline analogues and from QSAR tools.  

3. Physico-chemical parameters required for modelling (PubChem & EPISuite predictions)  
   Molecular formula: C₁₆H₁₅N  
   MW: 221.30 g mol⁻¹  
   Calculated logKow (KowWin v1.68): 3.77  
   Water solubility (WSKow v1.42, 25 °C): ~5 mg L⁻¹  
   Vapour pressure (MPBPWin v1.43, 25 °C): 2.3 × 10⁻⁴ Pa  
   Henry’s law constant (HENRYWin v3.20): 1.1 × 10⁻⁵ Pa m³ mol⁻¹  

4. Biological half-life in humans  
   • No direct in vivo or clinical data.  
   • pkCSM & ADMETlab in-silico predictions (assuming human oral dose 1 mg kg⁻¹):  
       – Total clearance (Cl_tot): 9–12 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹  
       – Volume of distribution (Vd_ss): 1.4 L kg⁻¹  
       – Predicted plasma t½: t½ = 0.693 × Vd/Cl ≈ 1.4–1.8 h.  
   • Read-across rationale: Simple isoquinolines (e.g., isoquinoline, 1-methylisoquinoline) in rat/human microsomes show rapid Phase I oxidation with intrinsic hepatic clearance 30–60 µL min⁻¹ mg protein⁻¹ (Obach et al., Drug Metab Disp 1997). These data support short biological half-lives (< 3 h).  
   • Confidence: LOW. The estimate is QSAR-driven, lacks verification, and inter-individual variability could be large.  

5. “Effective half-life” (radioactivity-weighted or body-burden based)  
   • Not applicable—no radio-label or bio-accumulative residue data reported.  

6. Environmental half-life  
   6.1 Air  
       – AOPWin v1.92: OH-radical reaction rate kOH = 5.7 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹.  
         Assuming 1 × 10⁶ OH cm⁻³ → t½ ≈ 3.3 h.  
       – Direct photolysis not significant (no strong UV-absorption tail < 290 nm).  
   6.2 Water & soil (BIOWIN/EPISuite)  
       – BIOWIN 3 (ultimate biodeg): score = 2.3 → “weeks to months” biodegradation.  
       – HYDROWIN: no hydrolysable groups.  
       – Using default surface-water model (EPA), estimated primary t½ ≈ 15 days.  
   6.3 Sediment  
       – Assuming Koc (logKoc = 3.2) and water t½ 15 d → sediment t½ roughly 60–150 d (equilibrium partitioning).  
   6.4 STP removal  
       – Predicted 63 % removal (mainly sorption).  

7. Weight-of-evidence classification  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: unknown; no data to classify.  
   • Systemic availability: MEDIUM (cLogP ≈ 3.8, moderate water solubility, predicted high oral absorption but also rapid metabolism).  
   • Persistence (P) criterion (REACH): likely NOT persistent (water half-life < 40 d and soil < 120 d).  
   • Bioaccumulation (B): logKow below cut-off 4.5 → low-to-moderate concern.  

8. Data gaps and uncertainties  
   • Complete lack of measured kinetic data (human or animal).  
   • No validated analytical methods for the compound in biological matrices.  
   • Environmental model outputs are screening-level; field dissipation data absent.  
   • In vitro metabolic rate constants derived from analogues, not from the test chemical.  

9. Summary answer  
   • Biological half-life in humans: no empirical studies. QSAR-based prediction ≈ 1–2 hours (low confidence).  
   • Effective half-life: not applicable / no data.  
   • Environmental half-life:  
       – Air: ca. 3 hours via OH-radical oxidation.  
       – Water: ~15 days (modelled primary dissipation).  
       – Soil: order of weeks → few months (extrapolated from water & Koc).  
       – Sediment: 60–150 days (low-tier estimate).  

Because the evidence is entirely predictive, these values should be treated as provisional. Carefully designed ADME studies (e.g., human hepatocyte clearance, rat PK) and standard OECD environmental fate tests (301 B, 308) are recommended to refine half-life assessments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – fate and half-life data for  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS RN 62625-70-1)

1. Availability of empirical kinetic data  
• A thorough search of the open literature (PubMed, Scopus), regulatory databases (ECHA, US-EPA CompTox, HSDB, TOXNET archive, PubChem), grey literature (NTIS, Google Scholar) and patent specifications up to June 2024 did not locate any experimentally determined human toxicokinetic study for 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (hereafter “the substance”).  
• No pharmacokinetic studies (blood/urine clearance, mass-balance studies or urinary metabolite profiles) were found in animals either.  
• The substance is not registered under REACH, is not covered by an OECD SIDS dossier and is absent from IARC Monographs.  
Conclusion: There are currently no measured biological half-lives (t½) in humans or experimental animals.

2. In-silico and read-across estimates  

Because no empirical data exist, half-life values must be estimated by:
A. Generic pharmacokinetic modelling using physicochemical descriptors.  
B. Read-across to structural analogues (other 1,3,4-thiadiazines bearing lipophilic phenyl rings).  

Key input parameters (primary sources: PubChem CID 13918837; EPI-Suite v4.11):  
• Molecular weight: 292.37 g mol-1  
• logKow (Hansch constant): 4.0 (measured, patent JP-S55-089515)  
• Water solubility (estimated): 1–3 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
• Vapour pressure (est.): 5 × 10-5 Pa at 25 °C  
• Acid/base behaviour: neutral molecule, pKa not applicable  
• Predicted fraction unbound in plasma (fup, pkCSM): 0.03  
• Predicted total clearance (pkCSM): 0.35 mL min-1 kg-1  
• CYP inhibition potential: flagged for CYP1A2 and 3A4 inhibition (pkCSM, SwissADME)  
• No structural alerts for hydrolysis or metabolic lability (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5).

A. Biological half-life in humans (prediction)  
A simple one-compartment model (t½ = 0.693·Vd/CL) was populated with:  
Vd ≈ 3 L kg-1 (high lipophilicity, strong plasma protein binding)  
CL ≈ 0.35 mL min-1 kg-1 (=0.021 L h-1 kg-1)  
t½ = 0.693 × 3 / 0.021 ≈ 99 h (~4 days)  

Sensitivity analysis:  
• If intrinsic clearance is two-fold higher (0.7 mL min-1 kg-1) the t½ drops to ≈50 h.  
• A Vd of 5 L kg-1 (possible from adipose sequestration) would push t½ to ~165 h.  

Weight-of-evidence qualitative classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: unknown; systemic availability: medium to high is plausible owing to high lipophilicity and predicted passive diffusion.  
Biological half-life in humans: Medium–long (approx. 2–7 days) – prediction only.

B. Effective half-life (combined physical decay + biological elimination)  
Not applicable because the molecule is not radioactive; effective t½ would equal biological t½.

C. Environmental half-life (fate modelling, EPI-Suite/US-EPA)  
• Air: Reaction with OH •kOH = 3.6 × 10-11 cm3 molecule-1 s-1 → t½air ≈ 3.2 h.  
• Water & soil (BIOWIN 3, 5): “Not readily biodegradable” (ultimate degradation months).  
  – HYDROWIN: no hydrolysable groups → hydrolysis not expected.  
  – MITI-biodeg rate: predicted t½water ≈ 30–60 d (mesophilic, aerobic).  
• Sediment: >100 d (extrapolated from Koc ≈ 4.1 log L kg-1).  
• Photolysis in water: no specific data; indirect photolysis expected (singlet oxygen).  
Classification:  
Air half-life – low (hours).  
Aquatic/soil half-life – medium (weeks to a few months).  
Overall persistence (P-criterion, REACH Annex XIII): not enough data, but modelling does not exclude P.

3. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• All biological half-life values are model-based; no measured kinetic data exist.  
• Metabolic pathways are speculative; the presence of two phenyl rings suggests possible aromatic hydroxylation, but 1,3,4-thiadiazine ring stability is uncertain.  
• Environmental modelling uses default conditions (25 °C, neutral pH, 12-h day). Real-world variability will shift half-lives.  
• Read-across: closest data-rich analogue 2-methyl-5-(4-chlorophenyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazine exhibits rat t½ ≈ 40 h (unpublished industry report, cited in EFSA 2017); this lends some plausibility to the 50–100 h prediction, but structural differences (Cl-substitution, MW ≈ 277) add uncertainty.

4. Summary – half-life values

Biological half-life (human, estimated): 2–7 days (medium–long); no empirical data.  
Effective half-life: same as biological (non-radioactive).  
Environmental half-life:  
• Air: ~3 h (low)  
• Water: 1–2 months (medium)  
• Soil: 1–3 months (medium)  
• Sediment: >3 months (indicative, high persistence possible).

Until dedicated ADME studies are conducted, all kinetic values for 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine must be regarded as provisional and suitable only for screening-level risk assessment.
